description: This course is designed to help graduates build a solid foundation in the financial system's structure and the different types of banks. Students will learn how to assess bank performance using CAMELS ratings and gain insights into both domestic and global banking regulations. The curriculum covers essential topics such as risk management practices, BASEL norms, and practical techniques for mitigating credit, operational, and market risks in real-world scenarios. Additionally, students will develop a comprehensive understanding of the credit appraisal process and the diverse array of financial products and services offered in both Retail and Investment banking sectors. They will also explore the roles of relationship managers and credit managers in ethical banking practices. Moreover, the course will delve into the significance of technology in banking operations, retail banking, and wealth management, including areas such as paytech, ATM services, regtech, and digital and mobile banking. Lastly, students will gain insights into cash management systems within the banking industry.
key_takeaways:
• Structural understanding of the financial system and the role of different bank types.
• Ability to assess bank performance using CAMELS ratings.
• Working knowledge of Basel I/II/III and capital-adequacy requirements.
• Understanding of credit appraisal processes for retail and corporate loans.
• Awareness of how technology is reshaping banking operations.
prerequisites:
• Basic familiarity with financial statements and the broad structure of financial markets.
• No deep quantitative prerequisite; course is managerial rather than technical.
• Core Financial Management helps with CAMELS and capital-adequacy discussions.
summary: Covers the structure of the financial system, types of banks, CAMELS-based performance assessment, Basel norms, and risk management in banking.
Includes credit appraisal, retail and investment banking products, relationship and credit-manager roles, and banking technology (paytech, regtech, digital banking).
Useful for managers targeting careers in banking, financial services, or corporate relationship roles that interact with banks.
Heavy on case studies and regulatory framework understanding.

complementary_courses.why: Direct continuation into risk-management techniques taught by the same faculty.
Covers how digital disruption is reshaping banking business models.
Deepens lender-side credit evaluation for larger corporate loans.

description: This course offers a comprehensive exploration of business valuation and analysis from multiple perspectives. Emphasis is placed on understanding and applying various valuation methodologies—including discounted cash flow (DCF) techniques, market multiples, real options, and industry‐specific approaches—to evaluate firms across different stages of their lifecycle. In doing so, the course examines the interplay between financial statement analysis, cost of capital estimation, and the identification of value drivers. Through lectures, case discussions, and hands‐on exercises, students will develop the analytical rigor necessary to critically assess and value business entities in dynamic financial environments.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to apply DCF, multiples, real options, and venture-capital valuation methods.
• Capability to identify value drivers from financial statements and forecasts.
• Understanding of IPO valuation and pre-IPO scenarios.
• Exposure to valuing companies with limited information (private firms, start-ups).
• Hands-on experience building integrated valuation models for listed Indian companies.
prerequisites:
• Strong grounding in Financial Management, accounting, and corporate finance.
• Excel proficiency is essential — most exercises involve real financial models.
• Pre-read of Peter Lynch's 'One Up on Wall Street' is recommended for developing the investor mindset.
course_curriculum:
Introduction to Business Analysis and Valuation
Comparative Valuation Approaches – FCF/ APV/ FTE/CCF  & Methods of Valuation
Some part to be taken by Industry Expert
Introduction — Industry Financing and Institutional Roles
summary: A rigorous course on business valuation: DCF methodologies (WACC/FCF, APV, FTE/CCF), market multiples, real options, and industry-specific approaches.
Integrates financial statement analysis, cost-of-capital estimation, and value-driver identification across firm lifecycles.
Includes a live-valuation module led by an industry expert covering IPO valuations, venture-capital methods, and valuation under limited information.
Core course for anyone heading into investment banking, equity research, PE/VC, corporate development, or strategic-finance roles.

complementary_courses.why: Valuation is the analytical backbone of all IB transactions.
Uses valuation as the core input for M&A deal-making.
Provides the Excel modelling rigour needed for any valuation exercise.

description:
This course covers key aspects of personal finance, including client risk profiling, investment avenues, goal-oriented investment planning, and insurance and retirement strategies.

Students will learn to assess individual financial needs, design tailored investment strategies, and analyze various financial products to optimize portfolio performance. The course also explores the impact of behavioral biases on financial decision-making and provides practical insights into managing risk and achieving financial security.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to run client risk profiling and build a goal-oriented financial plan.
• Working knowledge of equity, debt, mutual funds, alternative investments, and government schemes in the Indian context.
• Understanding of insurance, retirement planning, and tax-efficient structuring for individuals.
• Awareness of behavioural biases and how they distort investment decisions.
• Confidence to evaluate real-world financial products and explain trade-offs to non-expert audiences.
prerequisites:
• Comfort with Time Value of Money (TVM), compounding, and basic investment vocabulary (equity, debt, mutual funds).
• Familiarity with Indian investment avenues and tax basics is helpful but not mandatory.
• No formal prerequisite listed; a core Financial Management course makes the class easier to follow.
course_curriculum:
Introduction to wealth management
Financial Planning - Basics
Goal Oriented Investment Planning and Asset Rebalancing
Behavioural Finance & Emerging Trends
Taxation, Insurance and Retirement Planning
Analysis of Financial Products, Client Profiling & Marketing of Financial Product
Comprehensive Cases
Presentations
summary: Covers personal finance end-to-end: client risk profiling, goal-based investment planning, asset allocation, insurance, and retirement strategies.
Builds managerial capability to structure holistic financial plans under different life-stage and income scenarios.
Relevant for anyone who will advise clients, employees, or family on money decisions; also useful as a foundation for wealth-management and private-banking career paths.
Covers behavioural finance biases, so managers learn to separate rational planning from emotional decision-making.

complementary_courses.why: Extends personal planning into systematic securities analysis and portfolio construction.
Shows the institutional side of capital markets that retail clients interact with.
Deepens understanding of how clients actually make money decisions, reinforcing behavioural finance.

description: The global financial crisis and the current pandemic have highlighted the need to look at banking and financial institutions from a risk management perspective. The complexity of risks as a result of globalization and interconnectedness of banking and financial markets across countries has also changed significantly in the last few years. This course attempts to consider all aspects of risk management in financial markets that affect banks and other financial institutions operating there, including interest rate risk, liquidity risk, asset liability management, credit risk, operational risk, market risk, off-balance sheet risk, etc. It will attempt to measure and manage the risk management issues in banks and financial institutions.
key_takeaways:
• Comprehensive toolkit across interest-rate, liquidity, credit, market, and operational risks.
• Ability to apply risk-management techniques in bank and FI contexts.
• Capability to communicate the need for and impact of risk management to senior stakeholders.
• Understanding of regulatory frameworks (Basel) and their operational implications.
• Leadership perspective for aspiring CROs through case-based analysis.
prerequisites:
• Core Financial Management and foundational understanding of banking instruments.
• Comfort with quantitative analysis and basic statistics.
• Familiarity with derivatives (options, futures, swaps) is strongly recommended.
summary: Comprehensive coverage of risk in banks and financial institutions: interest-rate risk, liquidity risk, asset-liability management, credit, operational, market, and off-balance-sheet risks.
Case-driven course that uses post-GFC and pandemic-era examples to build risk intuition.
Useful for managers targeting roles as Chief Risk Officers, credit risk analysts, treasury specialists, or any corporate-finance role where hedging and risk policies matter.
Aligned to CFA / FRM candidate preparation through the Hull textbook.

complementary_courses.why: Same faculty, direct continuation with strong content overlap.
Derivatives are the primary hedging instruments in FRM.
Duration, convexity, and credit risk concepts feed directly into FRM.

description: The elective provides an overview of one of the most important markets in the financial world. Fixed income securities constitute the bulk of a financial institution’s assets as well as liabilities. The course will cover various types of fixed income securities and their unique features along with the reward-risk profiles. Participants will get a broad overview of the pricing method and risk computation methods along with an understanding of how market and credit risk affect valuations. Important fixed income securities portfolio will be discussed.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to price money-market and bond-market instruments accurately.
• Capability to compute and apply duration and convexity for risk management.
• Skills to construct and manage basic-to-intermediate bond portfolios.
• Understanding of valuing bonds with embedded options.
• Foundational exposure to interest-rate derivatives pricing and valuation.
prerequisites:
• Comfort with higher-secondary-level mathematics: algebra, basic calculus, linear algebra.
• Strong Excel skills (modelling, functions, what-if analysis) are essential — course explicitly requires practice.
• Core Financial Management and familiarity with TVM concepts are implicit prerequisites.
course_curriculum:
INTRODUCTION, SPOT RATES, FORWARD RATES, PAR RATES, YTM, CURRENT YIELD
MONEY MARKET & REPO
BOND MARKET
TERM STRUCTURE OF INTEREST RATES & INTERPOLATION
DURATION & CONVEXITY
MARKET RISK & CREDIT RISK
SECURITIZATION
BOND PORTFOLIO STRATEGIES
ARBITRAGE FREE VALUATION & PRICING BONDS WITH OPTIONS
FRA & INTEREST RATE FUTURES
INTEREST RATE SWAPS (IRS) – PRICING & VALUING IRS & CAPS & FLOORS
INTEREST RATE OPTIONS
summary: Deep dive into money and bond markets, covering spot/forward rates, YTM, duration, convexity, term structure, and credit/market risk.
Covers bond portfolio strategies, securitisation, arbitrage-free valuation, bonds with embedded options, and interest-rate derivatives (FRAs, swaps, caps, floors).
Essential for anyone headed into fixed-income trading, treasury management, credit ratings, or bank asset-liability management.
Heavily quantitative and Excel-driven; among the most technical Finance electives on the list.

complementary_courses.why: Derivatives coverage directly extends into interest-rate derivatives.
Applies fixed-income risk measures within a broader institutional risk context.
Banking balance sheets are dominated by fixed-income instruments.

key_takeaways:
• Ability to build a three-statement forecast model from historical data.
• Capability to perform ratio analysis, valuation, and control-panel dashboarding on forecasted statements.
• Understanding of sensitivity, scenario, and Monte Carlo approaches to risk analysis.
• Exposure to Vector Auto Regression (VAR) for multivariate time-series forecasting.
• A reusable toolkit of Excel templates for finance, consulting, and strategy work.
prerequisites:
• Comfort with Excel (formulas, references, basic functions) before the first session.
• Foundational knowledge of financial statements and how they link together.
• Core Financial Management serves as the conceptual base for the forecasting work.
course_curriculum:
Forecasting Financial Statements
Analysis of forecasted Financial statements
Uncertainty analysis using Sensitivity Analysis, Scenario Analysis, Monte Carlo Simulation
Multivariate Time Series Modelling- Vector Auto Regression (VAR)
Presentations
summary: A hands-on Excel course focused on building forecast models: integrated income statements, balance sheets, and cash flow statements.
Covers sensitivity analysis, scenario analysis, Monte Carlo simulation, and multivariate time-series modelling (VAR).
Highly practical skill-building course; outputs are directly usable in real valuation, strategy, or consulting engagements.
Short format (10 sessions) but tool-focused — you leave with reusable model templates.

complementary_courses.why: Direct application of modelling skills to valuation exercises.
Extends modelling into project finance and capital-budgeting contexts.
Complements Excel modelling with Python-based analytics for broader decision support.

description:
Businesses are operating in an increasingly interconnected global environment. Most businesses today are either directly or indirectly exposed to international competition. Managing such businesses requires an understanding of currency risks and the global financial environment. The objective of this course is to provide students with an in-depth knowledge of these issues. The main topics covered in this course are: forex markets, international Parity conditions, forex risks, currency derivatives and hedging issues, issues with currency investment strategies, issues with cross-border financing decisions and cross-border investment decisions.

Student Learning Outcomes:

After completing this course, students should be able to
1. Demonstrate basic understanding of the foreign exchange market and exchange rates
2. Demonstrate basic understanding of how to use foreign exchange derivatives and other techniques to manage foreign exchange exposures of firms
3. Demonstrate basic understanding of the issues of multinational financing and investment decisions
4. Demonstrate critical and analytical skills, wherein they should be able to make sense out of a mass of information to address relevant issues of international finance theory.
key_takeaways:
• Understanding of foreign exchange markets, spot/forward rates, and quotations.
• Ability to use forex derivatives to hedge currency exposure.
• Capability to evaluate international parity conditions (PPP, IRP, Fisher effect).
• Skills for cross-border capital budgeting and multinational financing decisions.
• Analytical framework for currency-risk management in MNC contexts.
prerequisites:
• Strong grounding in Financial Management and familiarity with corporate finance decisions.
• Basic macroeconomics, especially exchange rates and balance of payments.
• Comfort with numerical problem-solving and Excel-based hedging exercises.
course_curriculum:
Tutorial 1; Numerical problem solving involving topics covered in sessions 1-9 and Case discussion
International Capital Budgeting; Case/Minicase discussion
Financing International Trade; Case/Minicase discussion
Tutorial 2; Numerical problem solving involving topics covered in sessions 11-19 and Case discussion
summary: Covers forex markets, international parity conditions, currency derivatives, hedging, multinational financing and investment decisions, and cross-border capital budgeting.
Essential for managers in multinationals, exporters/importers, or corporate treasury functions dealing with currency exposure.
Research-paper heavy for students interested in academic rigour; also highly practical with numerical problem-solving sessions.
Particularly relevant given India's growing integration with global supply chains and capital markets.

complementary_courses.why: Derivatives coverage is the tool-set for forex hedging.
Provides the strategic business context for cross-border finance.
Macroeconomic foundation for exchange-rate and trade policy discussions.

description:
The course provides an understanding of the global paradigm of investment banking, the policy and market framework, business domain and activities and transaction advisory services.

It provides an in depth understanding of the intricacies and transactions in Core Investment Banking services provided by merchant bankers in India. It will also include discussions on   international capital market listings and methods.

The course will provide in-depth understanding of deal structuring, pricing, simulated modelling and transaction processes involved in various transactions as well as associated service aspects and regulatory framework in the Indian market with related comparatives in US markets.
key_takeaways:
• Clear understanding of IB as a capital-market service and its business model.
• Ability to analyse domestic issue management: listing decisions, pricing decisions, underwriting, and IPO modelling.
• Exposure to global capital-market offers, SPACs, and hard underwriting.
• Hands-on case exposure through Zomato, Paytm, Alibaba, Facebook, Spotify, and similar deals.
• Interview-ready vocabulary for IB, transaction advisory, and capital-market roles.
prerequisites:
• No formal prerequisite, but strong working knowledge of financial statements and corporate valuation is expected.
• Comfort with Excel-based financial modelling is essential.
• Prior exposure to capital markets through news or general awareness is strongly recommended.
summary: A practitioner-led course on the core investment banking business: issue management, IPOs, QIPs, buybacks, delistings, and international capital-market offerings.
Walks through deal structuring, pricing, and regulatory frameworks in Indian markets with comparative references to the US.
Useful for managers targeting careers in IB, transaction advisory, corporate development, equity research, or capital-market functions within any corporate.
Evaluation is heavily project-based (45% project, 50% end-term), so expect deep engagement with real-world deals.

complementary_courses.why: Covers the buy-side lens that IB professionals must understand.
Deepens valuation rigour with DCF, multiples, and real options.
Extends IB deal-making into M&A transaction execution.

description: The course will enable students to understand the various aspects pertaining to Mergers & Acquisition and Corporate Restructuring with focus on the financial, legal and strategic aspects.
key_takeaways:
• Comprehensive understanding of M&A types, motives, and theoretical frameworks.
• Ability to identify and evaluate target companies using valuation techniques.
• Capability to analyse takeover tactics, defenses, and deal structures.
• Skills in financial modelling for M&A transactions.
• Awareness of legal, tax, and accounting implications in deal structuring.
prerequisites:
• Corporate Finance and Financial Accounting are explicit prerequisites.
• Excel modelling skills are required for valuation and deal-structuring exercises.
• Prior exposure to valuation concepts (DCF, multiples) helps.
course_curriculum:
Understanding of Corporate Re-structuring: Forms,
Acquisition Tactics and Takeover Defenses
M&A Strategy fit,
Business Valuation using discounted cash flow valuation methodology
Relative Valuation Methodologies,
Application of Financial Modeling Techniques to M&A
Analyzing Privately Held                                                                                                                                                                 Companies and Start up Valuation
Deal Structuring , Modes of Financing, Tax implications, legal aspects and accounting for M&A, in M&A
Project presentation
summary: Financial, legal, and strategic perspectives on M&A and corporate restructuring.
Covers business combinations, takeover tactics and defenses, LBOs, MBOs, synergy analysis, DCF/relative valuation for M&A, and integration challenges.
Includes financial modelling for M&A, deal structuring, and tax/legal implications — highly practical for corporate-development and IB careers.
Uses live cases including the HUL-GSK Consumer Healthcare merger and start-up valuations.

complementary_courses.why: Core valuation toolkit that M&A transactions depend on.
Strategic rationale for M&A within corporate strategy.
complementary_courses.faculty: Prof. Nilesh Khare & Prof. Aarti Singh

complementary_courses.why: M&A transactions are structured and financed by investment banks; this course provides the deal origination, valuation, and capital markets context that underpins every M&A transaction.

description:
The financial derivatives market has experienced remarkable growth in recent years, playing an increasingly vital role in risk management and investment strategies. Modern managers and financial professionals utilize derivatives such as futures, options, and swaps to hedge against various types of risks, enhance portfolio performance, and optimize financial decision-making.

This course is designed to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of derivative instruments, their valuation, and their practical applications in financial markets. Through a blend of theoretical foundations, real-world case studies, and hands-on problem-solving, students will develop the skills needed to analyze, price, and employ derivatives effectively. Emphasis will be placed on understanding market mechanisms, trading strategies, and risk management techniques, ensuring that students gain both the conceptual knowledge and practical expertise required to navigate the evolving landscape of financial derivatives.

This course will be delivered through a balanced mix of lectures, case discussions, and problem-solving exercises to provide a comprehensive understanding of options, futures, and derivative markets. Real-world financial data and corporate disclosures will be examined to demonstrate practical applications and enhance analytical skills.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to describe and operate in futures, options, swaps, and credit-derivative markets.
• Capability to price derivatives using binomial, BSM, and Heston models.
• Proficiency in designing hedging strategies using futures and options.
• Understanding of the Greeks (delta, gamma, vega, theta) and their use in risk management.
• Introduction to Value at Risk (VaR), volatility smiles, and exotic option structures.
prerequisites:
• Strong analytical aptitude and comfort with probability, calculus, and basic stochastic concepts.
• General understanding of financial markets and instruments.
• Excel modelling comfort is important for problem-solving sessions.
course_curriculum:
Introduction to Derivatives
Mechanics of Futures Markets
Interest Rates; Forward & Futures Pricing
Interest Rate Futures
Hedging with Futures
Case Discussion: Application for Financial Futures
Mechanics of Option Markets
Properties of Stock Options
Option Trading Strategies
Wiener Process & Martingales
Binomial & BSM Models
Advanced Option Pricing
The Greek Letters
Volatility Estimation
Value at Risk
Exotic Options
Credit Derivatives
Swaps
Tutorial & Review
summary: Builds a rigorous understanding of derivative instruments — futures, options, swaps, and exotic derivatives — and their use in hedging and risk management.
Combines theoretical foundations (Black-Scholes-Merton, binomial models, Greeks, stochastic calculus) with trading strategies and real-world hedging cases.
Essential for managers in treasury, risk, trading, or corporate finance where hedging currency, commodity, or interest-rate risk is part of the job.
Quantitatively demanding: volatility modelling, VaR, and interest-rate derivatives are covered in depth.

complementary_courses.why: Core equity/portfolio context that derivatives are used to hedge.
Extends derivatives thinking into interest-rate and bond markets.
Applies derivative concepts to institutional risk management (Basel, credit/market risk).

description: Projects are generally financed, as a good measure, with a combination of owned and borrowed funds. Project formulation, financing and execution is a big team work with lot of uncertainties and risks. No doubt, project promoters, lenders and consultants are the key players in this domain and this course will take one through the start of Project Characteristics to Stages of Project Development, Feasibility studies and Formulation as well as evaluation of the Projects. The course will deal with all types of projects pertaining to Infrastructure, Industrial, Service Sector and International projects.  The long term demand for capital and infrastructure investment remains large, which implies the long-term prospects for project finance remain strong. Needless to say, successful businesses, banks and economy, at large, get built up on successful projects only. Given the magnitude of current investment and prospects for future growth, financial managers, bankers and government officials need to understand what project finance is, why it creates value, and how to structure transactions that have a high probability of succeeding both operationally and financially.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to structure and model a project from conception through implementation.
• Understanding of the lender's perspective: due diligence, CMA data, credit-risk assessment.
• Exposure to international project finance and syndicated lending strategies.
• Working knowledge of Social Cost-Benefit Analysis (SCBA) for infrastructure projects.
• Capability to identify, assess, and mitigate project risks across the lifecycle.
prerequisites:
• Strong foundation in Financial Management, especially capital budgeting.
• Comfort with Excel for financial modelling during hands-on exercises.
• Basic understanding of financial statement analysis and ratio interpretation.
course_curriculum:
Infrastructure Projects- BOT/BOOT Model;  Different types of PPP Structures, Parties involved with their  responsibilities
Formulation of Project : Learn modelling of Project – Manufacturing
Viewing Projects from different basis of Cash Flows
Infrastructure Project - Sason Power Project
Constraints in Sources of Finance: Importance of Leverage Ratio, Debt Service Coverage and Margin to finalize the Means of Finance
Financial products available and various schemes for Project Financing; Debt and Equity of various types
Viability, Project Planning & Implementation : How to go about a Project
Lender’s Perspective in Project Financing : Due diligence and study of bankability of the projects, Facets of Project appraisal: Management, Technical, Marketing, Financial & Economic
Lender’s Perspective Continued : Due Diligence: CMA Data a necessary tool in financial analysis used by Lenders
Sanctions, Documentation, Disbursement and Monitoring of projects
Introduction to International Projects; Ease of doing business and Starting a new business in a foreign country
Stressed units and NPAs, and its Revival - Definition, Identification, Classification, Government Initiatives and Revival Strategies and Guide to insolvency and Bankruptcy Code
Social Cost-Benefit Analysis of Projects
Risk Analysis of Projects : Categories of Project risks and their mitigation &
summary: Covers the full project-financing lifecycle: capex decisions, project formulation, feasibility, appraisal, credit risk rating, and syndicated lending.
Industry-focused course with strong practitioner content — BOT/BOOT models, infrastructure projects, CMA data, Basel norms, and stressed-asset resolution.
Highly relevant for managers in infrastructure, banking, corporate finance, or project-led businesses where large capital commitments are routine.
Live-case teaching with projects like the Sasan Power project and Chase syndicated lending.

complementary_courses.why: Valuation frameworks directly support project-appraisal decisions.
Extends project risk into institutional risk-management practices.
Lender-side view that complements borrower-side project structuring.

description: The principal objective of this course is to endow students with a comprehensive understanding of the theories, models, and practical nuances of security analysis and portfolio management. The course is structured into three modules: (i) the investment ecosystem, (ii) security analysis covering both fundamental and technical perspectives alongside market efficiency and behavioral finance aspects, and (iii) portfolio management strategies including risk-return dynamics, diversification, optimization, and evaluation. Students will also gain preliminary exposure to fixed-income securities, derivative instruments, and hedging mechanisms. Pedagogy comprises a blend of lectures, problem-solving sessions, case studies, real-data-based projects, and assignments. Students are expected to actively engage in these activities for optimal learning outcomes.
key_takeaways:
• Structural understanding of domestic and global financial markets and securities.
• Ability to run both fundamental and technical analyses on equity securities.
• Capability to build and manage diversified portfolios using modern portfolio theory.
• Grasp of EMH, behavioural finance, and how market anomalies are exploited.
• Preliminary exposure to fixed income, derivatives, and hedging within a portfolio context.
prerequisites:
• Financial Management I & II (core courses) and Financial Accounting are explicit prerequisites.
• Comfort with Excel and basic statistics for data-heavy sessions.
• Basic awareness of Indian and global capital markets helps contextualise class discussions.
course_curriculum:
Module 1: The Investment Ecosystem: Market structures, players, capital and money markets, Primary and Secondary Markets
The Investment Ecosystem: Market structures, players, capital and money markets, Primary and Secondary Markets
Module 2: Security Analysis: Fundamental Analysis, Technical Analysis, EMH, Behavioral Finance
Security Analysis: Fundamental Analysis, Technical Analysis, EMH, Behavioral Finance
Tutorial 1
Portfolio Mathematics: Risk and Return concepts, Diversification, Efficient Frontier, Optimal Portfolio
Tutorial 2
Portfolio Management: Asset Allocation, Active and Passive Management, Debt Portfolio Management, Portfolio Performance Evaluation
Tutorial 3 and/or Project Presentation with Viva
summary: A foundational investments course covering the full investment ecosystem: fundamental analysis, technical analysis, Efficient Market Hypothesis, behavioural finance, and portfolio construction.
Deep coverage of risk-return theory, diversification, efficient frontier, optimal portfolios, asset allocation, active vs passive management, and performance evaluation.
Useful for anyone who will manage investments — personally, professionally, or as part of a corporate treasury — and for those preparing for equity research or portfolio management roles.
Uses real market data and hands-on projects to build practical analytical rigour.

complementary_courses.why: Derivatives are a core tool for portfolio hedging and enhancement.
Valuation rigour directly supports fundamental equity analysis.
Rounds out the equity-heavy SAPM content with debt instruments.

description: This course introduces students to responsible and evidence-based trading practices in modern financial markets. The course integrates technical analysis, fundamental screening models, and ethical decision-making, enabling students to design trading strategies that are both profitable and aligned with professional integrity. Students will learn to interpret price action, chart patterns, and momentum indicators, while also applying fundamental scoring models such as Piotroski F-Score, Beneish M-Score, Montier C-Score, and ownership analysis. The course emphasizes avoiding market manipulation, insider-based decisions, excessive speculation, and socially harmful trading practices. Through hands-on exercises, case discussions, and a strategy development project, students develop actionable trading frameworks and learn how Artificial Intelligence can support research and pattern identification. The course prepares students to approach financial markets with discipline, transparency, and ethical responsibility.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to interpret price action, chart patterns, and momentum indicators to form trading hypotheses.
• Capability to apply forensic screening models (Piotroski, Beneish, Montier) to filter corporate quality.
• Skills to design and back-test systematic trading strategies with entry/exit rules and risk controls.
• Framework for integrating ethical considerations into trading decisions.
• Awareness of how AI can support pattern identification while avoiding speculative overreach.
prerequisites:
• No formal prerequisite listed; foundation in Financial Management and basic investments is implicit.
• Comfort with reading financial statements for fundamental scoring work.
• Excel skills help with back-testing and scoring-model construction.
course_curriculum:
Introduction to Technical Analysis
Understanding Events and Price Action
Candle Stick Analysis
Moving Averages
Relative Strength Index (RSI)
Volume analysis
Ownership Pattern Analysis
Framework with Pattern Identification and decoding
Fundamental analysis and Leveraging AI
Student strategy Evaluation and presentation
summary: Combines technical analysis, fundamental screening, and ethical decision-making to design responsible trading strategies.
Covers candlestick patterns, moving averages, RSI, volume analysis, and fundamental scoring models (Piotroski F-Score, Beneish M-Score, Montier C-Score).
Emphasises avoidance of market manipulation, insider trading, excessive speculation, and socially harmful trading practices.
Useful for managers entering equity markets personally or professionally, where discipline and ethics matter as much as returns.

complementary_courses.why: Core investment analysis framework that trading strategies apply.
Covers cognitive biases that drive unethical trading behaviour.
complementary_courses.faculty: Prof. Shikha Bhardwaj & Prof. Diptiranjan Mahapatra

complementary_courses.why: Brings AI and NLP tools into financial analysis.
complementary_courses.course: AI for Financial Analytics and Decision Making

description:
The emergence of Agentic AI—autonomous systems capable of goal formulation, planning, tool usage, execution, and self-improvement—marks a shift from AI as a decision-support tool to AI asss an operational actor within organizations.

This course focuses on the managerial design, governance, and deployment of AI agents in business environments. Rather than learning AI tools, students will learn how to conceptualize AI agents, structure multi-agent workflows, manage human–AI collaboration, evaluate business ROI, and design AI-first organizations.

The course adopts a no-code managerial perspective, emphasizing strategy frameworks, simulations, case studies, and decision labs instead of technical implementation.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to distinguish automation, generative AI, and agentic AI systems in organisational contexts.
• Capability to design goal-driven AI agent architectures for managerial decisions.
• Framework for evaluating business feasibility and ROI of autonomous agents.
• Skills to lead human-AI hybrid teams effectively.
• Understanding of governance, ethics, and regulatory frameworks for agentic systems.
prerequisites:
• Basic understanding of AI and analytics at a managerial level.
• Familiarity with business decision-making processes.
• Prior exposure to digital transformation concepts is preferred but not mandatory.
course_curriculum:
What is Agentic AI? From Generative AI to Agentic AI — The Shift to Autonomous Systems
Agentic AI Architecture for Managers (Conceptual): Goals, Tools, Memory, Feedback
Managerial Decision-Making with AI Agents
Multi-Agent Coordination in Organizations
Multi-agent supply chain system
AI Agents and Organizational Power Structures
Autonomous AI Agents in Financial Decision-Making and Risk Governance
Human–AI Collaboration: Leadership, Trust & Control
Governance and Responsible Management of Autonomous AI Agents
Agentic AI Deployment Plan
Measuring ROI & Performance of Agentic AI
Industry Guest Lecture 1: Agentic AI in Large Enterprises
Industry Guest Lecture 2: Startups & AI-Native Business Models
Industry Guest Lecture 3: Strategic problem analysis
Student Presentations: Strategic Agentic AI Deployment Plan
summary: Managerial course on autonomous AI systems — how they plan, use tools, execute, and self-improve without constant human oversight.
No-code orientation focused on designing AI agent architectures, multi-agent workflows, human-AI collaboration, and AI-first organisational design.
Covers governance, ethics, regulatory frameworks, ROI measurement, and organisational transformation caused by agentic AI.
Essential for any manager who will deploy, lead, or oversee AI agents as operational actors in their business.

complementary_courses.why: Conceptual AI foundation that this course assumes.
GenAI product-management module directly extends into agentic thinking.
complementary_courses.faculty: Ms. Ankita Parihar & Mr. Ramkumar Arumugam

description:
Big Data Analytics has become a core managerial capability for organizations seeking competitive advantage in dynamic and uncertain environments. Managers today must go beyond intuition and rely on data-driven insights to formulate strategies, optimize operations, enhance customer experience, and manage risks.

This course adopts a management and decision-making orientation, focusing on how managers can frame business problems, evaluate analytics-driven solutions, interpret results, and translate insights into strategic actions. Technical depth is deliberately limited; emphasis is placed on managerial judgment, value creation, governance, ethics, and organizational readiness. The pedagogy relies heavily on Harvard Business School (HBS) case studies and industry guest lectures.
key_takeaways:
• Understanding of the strategic importance of Big Data Analytics in decision-making.
• Ability to critically analyse business problems using analytics-based reasoning.
• Capability to evaluate Big Data initiatives on business value, risk, and ROI.
• Framework for integrating analytics insights with strategic management theories.
• Awareness of ethical, legal, and governance principles in data-driven decisions.
prerequisites:
• Management Information Systems and an introductory Business Analytics course are recommended.
• No technical programming required.
• Comfort with reading quantitative data and interpreting HBS-style cases.

description: Increased digitization of business processes has resulted in explosive growth in the amount of structured and unstructured data collected by firms. description:
This course is an introduction to the business of cloud computing, designed specifically for management students. In today’s rapidly evolving technology landscape, understanding the strategic, operational, and financial implications of cloud adoption is imperative for future managers. Accordingly, focus has been laid on cloud business models, cloud economics, go-to-market strategies and decision-making frameworks.

This course aims to equip future managers with the analytical and strategic skill sets required to evaluate, plan and manage cloud initiatives in real-world business contexts. Learning will be enhanced through a flipped classroom-based pedagogy, combining case studies, simulations and interactive discussions to bridge technology concepts with business and financial decision-making.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to analyse and evaluate cloud computing strategies.
• Understanding of cloud service and deployment models across industries.
• Capability to build cloud ROI/TCO models for business decisions.
• Framework for cloud GRC — governance, risk, compliance.
• Skills to design cloud-native go-to-market strategies for products and services.
prerequisites:
• Basic familiarity with enterprise IT concepts (servers, applications, databases).
• Comfort with financial analysis for ROI/TCO exercises.
• No formal prerequisite; managerial orientation throughout.
summary: Management-focused introduction to cloud computing: strategy, economics, go-to-market, and decision frameworks for cloud adoption.

description: Cyber Security for Business examines cybersecurity as a strategic organizational risk and governance priority rather than merely a technical concern. The course equips MBA students with a managerial understanding of the evolving cyber threat landscape, foundational information security principles (CIA triad, defense-in-depth, zero trust, IAM), cyber risk management frameworks (NIST, ISO 27001), breach case analysis, incident response planning, business continuity, and regulatory compliance. It also explores emerging challenges in AI-driven threats, IoT and cloud security, banking and e-commerce vulnerabilities, and cyber governance in domestic and international contexts. Through case discussions, structured risk analysis, and a cybersecurity simulation exercise, students develop the capability to evaluate organizational cyber readiness, participate in crisis decision-making, and contribute meaningfully to board-level cybersecurity strategy.
key_takeaways:
• Understanding of the cybersecurity threat landscape and common attack vectors.
• Ability to assess and prioritise enterprise IT risks.
• Framework for applying governance standards like NIST and ISO 27001.
• Capability to design incident-response and business-continuity plans.
• Awareness of regulatory compliance and data-privacy obligations.
prerequisites:

description:
Software has become highly pervasive. There is hardly any industry that is not increasingly dependent on software, be it as part of their products or as the backbone of their business operations. A good example of that phenomenon is the automobile industry – the software contents of cars have increased exponentially over the last decade. In more and more industries software product /components are turning into the number one value driver. Companies, with software intensive products driven digital business models have been disrupting the time and tested businesses in every industry. To help the students who are keen to pursue careers in the product ecosystem as product managers, or who intend to take the entrepreneurial plunge with digital products and platforms, International Software Product Management Association (ISPMA), the global not-for-profit body of product management experts from both the industry and academia has published a detailed syllabus – Product Management (https://ispma.org/curriculum/ SPM – The Foundation). For those students, professionals as well as the founders who wish to go for a formal certification, ISQI, an independent certification body facilitates remote proctored examination and certification (https://ispma.org/certification/ ).

The objective of this course is to promote general understanding of the discipline of product management for digital businesses and software products including the management of software parts of software-intensive products, i.e. systems or services.

Along with ISPMA’s startup product management syllabus, the GenAI product Management Modules will equip MBA students with the skills to design, build, and scale AI-powered products in Indian and emerging market contexts. The focus is on product thinking, decision-making, tooling, metrics, governance, and go-to-market strategy.

This syllabus, modeled on the ISPMA’s startup product management syllabus, covers the full spectrum of elements of software product management that are well-supported by literature and industrial practice. The syllabus is the basis for examination to certify that the examinee has achieved the degree of knowledge described in this syllabus. The terms used in this syllabus are consistent with the glossary of the ISPMA.

Included and excluded key areas:

The syllabus covers knowledge applicable for any kind of software intensive digital startups business contexts. This syllabus, however, does not provide for specialization in any specific domain areas (e.g fintech or IoT) - it rather describes the base knowledge necessary, which can be complemented with domain specific learning through live intern projects.

The syllabus is independent of any specific process model, and thus defines knowledge of a software product manager without prescribing exact interfaces to other roles in a product organization.

This course enables a student to play any of the following roles in the digital startups and their ecosystem - Product Manager, founder / management roles in digital startups, governance and facilitation roles in digital startup ecosystem like accelerations, incubators, venture capital investment firms.
key_takeaways:
• End-to-end product management using the ISPMA framework.
• Business Model Canvas and business case construction for digital products.
• Ability to distinguish AI-native products from traditional SaaS and identify high-value AI use cases.
• Hands-on familiarity with the modern GenAI and automation toolchain.
• Frameworks for AI governance, trust, and evaluation.
prerequisites:
• No formal prerequisite, but heavy workload (~60 hrs outside class across 10 weeks).
• Comfort with Business Model Canvas and basic agile/scrum vocabulary.
• Openness to hands-on tool experimentation in the GenAI bootcamp.
• Explicitly *not* suited for those seeking only entry-level PM skills or retail/category PM roles.

Role of Requirements Engineering in Software Product Management

Group Project Presentations and Q&A

description:
The Internet today has permeated all walks of life including commerce and business. The Internet has a dramatic impact on how individuals and organizations transact goods and services. It has given rise to new organizational forms, e-markets for physical and digital goods and services, and a variety of new digital business models. These developments have potentially profound implications for organizations and society as a whole. Further, there is currently a phenomenal interest in start-ups in India in this space.

With start-ups like Flipkart, Snapdeal and the like commanding billion dollar plus valuations, the interest in this area is going up. Unfortunately, while some of these start-ups succeed, many fall by the roadside. This could be due to several reasons – lack of funding, strategic issues, too much competition, incorrect focus, etc. This course will provide the students with an overall understanding of the emerging world of e- commerce.

description:
In today's volatile business landscape, digital disruption has evolved from a periodic threat to a continuous force reshaping entire industries. While technologies like artificial intelligence, blockchain, cloud computing, and the Internet of Things dominate headlines as transformative solutions, the reality of digital transformation is far more nuanced and complex. This course moves beyond the technology hype to examine the strategic, organizational, and human dimensions of successful digital transformation. Digital transformation represents a fundamental rethinking of how organizations use technology, people, and processes to radically change business performance. Despite significant investments—estimated at over $1.3 trillion annually—research indicates that approximately 70% of digital transformation initiatives fail to achieve their objectives. This course is designed to help students understand why transformations fail and what separates successful digital leaders from laggards. Through an intensive 20-session format combining strategic frameworks, contemporary case studies, industry practitioner insights, and hands-on project work, you will develop critical capabilities to assess, design, and execute digital transformation initiatives. The course emphasizes application over theory—you'll learn not just what digital transformation is, but how to actually make it happen in complex organizational contexts.

This course is particularly relevant in the era of Generative AI, where the pace of technological change is accelerating. Rather than treating any single technology as a panacea, we examine how successful organizations integrate multiple technologies into coherent transformation strategies while managing the inherent risks, ethical considerations, and organizational resistance that accompany major change initiatives.
key_takeaways:
• Framework for diagnosing digital transformation readiness in organisations.
• Ability to design digital strategy aligned with business objectives.
• Understanding of change-management challenges specific to digital transformation.
• Capability to evaluate technology investments and operating-model redesigns.
• Awareness of common failure modes in enterprise digital initiatives.
prerequisites:
• Familiarity with core management concepts including strategy and organisational behaviour.
• Basic understanding of digital technologies (cloud, data, mobile) is helpful.
• No deep technical prerequisite — course is managerial in orientation.

description:
Sports analytics has evolved from being a niche curiosity to a core pillar of modern sports ecosystems. Today, every major sporting organization, league, and athlete relies on data-driven insights to gain a competitive edge. Sports KPI continues to play an integral role in this transformation—helping teams and federations improve performance, enhance decision-making, and implement end-to-end analytical solutions that merge technology, strategy, and science.

The business potential of analytics in sports has expanded dramatically in recent years. It now spans performance optimization, player recruitment, fan engagement, injury prevention, and even sustainability management. The rapid adoption of AI, machine learning, and real-time IoT tracking has reshaped how games are played, coached, and consumed.

Think back to how we tracked the 2023 Cricket World Cup—simple metrics such as top scorers or highest run rates dominated the conversation. Fast-forward to 2025, and analytics now dives much deeper: ball-by-ball win probability models, AI-generated performance visualizations, and predictive fatigue monitoring are commonplace across cricket, football, tennis, basketball, and beyond. Media platforms and franchises alike now deliver interactive, data-rich insights that transform how fans experience the game.

By the end of this course, aspiring sports managers will gain hands-on exposure to cutting-edge analytical techniques and AI-driven tools that mirror the technologies used by global sports franchises. They will be equipped not only to interpret data but also to apply it meaningfully—to elevate both athletic performance and organizational strategy in the evolving world of sports.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to apply data-driven decision-making to sports performance and business questions.
• Understanding of player-valuation and team-strategy models.
• Exposure to sports media, broadcasting, and sponsorship analytics.
• Framework for fan engagement and retention analytics.
• Transferable skills in building predictive models under constrained datasets.
prerequisites:
• Introductory Business Analytics or statistical comfort is recommended.
• General interest in sports helps with case engagement.
• No formal prerequisite listed.
summary: Applies analytics and data science to sports decisions: player performance, team strategy, fan engagement, broadcasting, and sports business management.

description:
This course is designed for students interested in information technology, IT consulting, and managing organizations undergoing digital transformation. As businesses seek to leverage IT for strategic advantage, the role of IT consultants becomes critical in helping organizations articulate their needs, navigate technological constraints, and develop clear, actionable strategies. This course familiarizes students with both the content—key concepts, industry practices, and emerging trends in IT consulting—and the process, covering everything from proposal development and scope definition to contract execution and closure.

A core component of IT consulting is business analysis, which enables organizations to make informed, strategic decisions in a fast-paced digital landscape. This course equips students with essential business analysis skills, including identifying business needs, evaluating solutions, and optimizing processes. Through real-world case studies, hands-on exercises, and industry-standard techniques, students will learn to bridge the gap between business objectives and technology solutions. Whether working on digital transformation, process improvement, or enterprise strategy, students will gain the expertise needed to deliver high-impact, value-driven solutions in dynamic, multicultural business environments. This course will provide skills relevant for Business Analysts, Technology Consultants, Product Managers and Product Designers.

description:
The course is designed to familiarize students with the strategic, analytical, and execution-oriented dimensions of sales and selling management, with a strong emphasis on their application in real-world business contexts. It provides a holistic understanding of how contemporary sales functions operate across industries and organizational settings, while responding to rapidly evolving market conditions and customer expectations. The course goes in-depth into core sales and selling management, with special focus on the use of sales technologies that includes artificial intelligence (AI)-enabled tools in sales decision-making. They will engage with practical sales situations such as qualifying prospects, personalizing sales proposals, and optimizing the sales cycle. Students will learn how to use sales technologies to analyze buying behavior, predict market trends, and automate routine administrative tasks, enabling them to focus on higher-value sales activities. Through sales negotiation simulations and real-world scenarios, students will learn how sales technologies can enhance and transform the sales process.

This course combines proven sales techniques with the latest sales-enabled technologies. The course takes an interdisciplinary approach to sales problem-solving, drawing on concepts from marketing, analytics, psychology, operations, and technology to improve selling processes and performance. Experiential learning is central to the course. Students work closely with a startup through a live project to understand its sales process, identify gaps, and design practical improvements. Learning is anchored in real-world contexts through experience-based case studies, live business problems, role plays, and hands-on projects, with a strong emphasis on the diverse industry environment.

description:
Price, introduced as one of the 4 Ps of marketing and most management graduate learn this critical variable in cursory way. description:
Sales and Distribution is a critical course for future marketers, business strategists, and entrepreneurs, as it addresses two core pillars of market execution: how firms reach their customers (distribution) and how they persuade them to act (sales). Together, these functions form the lifeline of revenue generation and market expansion, and their strategic alignment is often the difference between success and stagnation in competitive marketplaces.

This course is designed to help MBA students understand, evaluate, and manage the key decisions associated with sales force effectiveness and distribution network design, both of which demand distinct yet complementary capabilities. While the sales dimension focuses on managing relationships, structuring sales teams, sales allocation, designing incentives, and executing go-to-market strategies, the distribution dimension deals with managing value chains, choosing and coordinating channel partners, optimizing logistics, and adapting distribution architecture to different customer segments and geographies.

The course adopts a dual-track teaching approach:

The Sales module explores the selling process, customer acquisition and retention strategies, territory and quota allocation, sales technologies, salesforce recruitment, training and motivation.
The Distribution module delves into channel structure and design, partner relationship management, retail and wholesale dynamics, last-mile challenges, omni-channel strategies, and innovations in physical and digital distribution systems.

While the two modules are taught as distinct managerial domains to allow students to deeply understand their principles and operational logic, they are consistently brought into dialogue with each other. Through case studies, industry insights and in-class exercises, students will learn to navigate the interdependence between sales execution and distribution strategy. The ultimate outcome is to prepare the students to excel at the sales and distribution jobs (typically their first job!).
key_takeaways:
• Comprehensive understanding of sales-management challenges across business types.
• Ability to make decision-making variables in distribution channel design.
• Framework for sales territory management, quota setting, and compensation.
• Skills in channel conflict resolution and relationship management.
• Awareness of rural distribution challenges and omnichannel evolution.
prerequisites:
• Core Marketing Management foundations are implicit.
• Openness to role-plays and simulations.
course_curriculum:
Salesforce Automation
Salesforce Performance Management and Sales Ethics
Managing Channel Performance & Relationships
Logistics Management
Channel Economics
Rural Distribution
Retailing and e-tailing

Covers channel design, conflict management, rural distribution, logistics, and omnichannel strategies.

description:

As a result, the traditional principles of marketing developed during the 20th century are also being challenged and revised to embrace sustainability as a core business component. Sustainability marketing is a powerful tool that helps to multiply the sustainable efforts of companies and businesses. It is the process of creating and delivering value to customers in a manner that respects or enhances both the environment and society.
Students in this course will learn about the principles of sustainability marketing across various industries and gain a deep understanding of the sustainable consumer.
It analyzes an organization's opportunities to develop sustainability practices related to product development, pricing, supply and distribution channels, promotion, and target markets.

description:
This course is designed to provide students with an understanding of the fundamentals of luxury. When was the concept of luxury first articulated, and what did it mean within its various manifestations? How did the products, consumer tastes, material exchanges, and producer strategies evolve through time? What is the state of the luxury industry today, and what is at its core? Building on cutting-edge economics and marketing research and organizational and strategic insights on the role of status and style in consumer behaviours and business decisions, students will be able to hone critical thinking and managerial skills related to planning and executing effective luxury and premium strategies.

The curriculum is designed to reflect the latest trends in luxury brand development, business management and market strategy, ensuring that you gain the skills and knowledge required to succeed in any part of the luxury sector including fashion, beauty, lifestyle, wellness, travel, culture or more.

description:
Rural markets (often conceptualized as base-of-the-pyramid markets) serve around 908.8 million Indian consumers (70% of Indian population) and
contribute to 53% of FMCG sales and 59% of consumer durables sales in the country. With the affluent markets nearly saturated, the competition has started to intensify in the base-of-the-pyramid (BoP) markets. Despite their significance to the Indian economy, rural markets remain marred with challenges starting from socio-economic deprivation, low-literacy to poor infrastructure and institutional gaps. While scholars and practitioners have made impressive advances in the past two decades, poverty alleviation remains elusive. To unlock marketings true force, it is important to
develop a deep understanding of poverty, rural market actors, application of marketing mix at the BoP, institutional environment, and the roles of different stakeholders. Therefore, this course is uniquely crafted to cultivate informed empathy alongside business skills to tackle market challenges. The course adopts a multi-format teaching pedagogy that combines lecture-based teaching with simulations, virtual immersion, case studies and video essays. Notably, the course involves a field immersion, where students visit rural markets and households to observe market actors in the natural settings of their market or homes, and conduct participatory rural appraisal (PRA) to understand their needs and wants.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to identify rural business opportunities with community co-benefits.
• Framework for the 4A rural marketing mix (Awareness, Acceptability, Affordability, Availability).
• Understanding of rural product, pricing, distribution, and communications.
• Skills in Participatory Rural Appraisal and ethnographic research.
• Awareness of the institutional environment and stakeholder roles at BoP.
prerequisites:
• Marketing Management I & II (core courses) are explicit prerequisites.
• Openness to field immersion and ethnographic methods.
• Basic sensitivity to socio-economic context of rural India.
course_curriculum:
Understanding Poverty
Rural Markets and Consumption
Creating V
Product Strategies
Pricing for Rural Markets
Reaching the
Marketing communications at the Base-of-the-pyramid
Partnerships and Rural Marketing Environment
summary: Deep-dive into rural markets (908+ million Indian consumers) with a focus on base-of-pyramid marketing, affordability, and institutional gaps.
Includes field immersion in rural households and Participatory Rural Appraisal (PRA) techniques.
Covers product, pricing, distribution, and communications strategies specifically for rural contexts.
Essential for managers at FMCG, consumer durables, agritech, microfinance, or any business targeting rural India.

description:
Building long-term relationships with customers is now accepted as an overriding goal of marketing and business as a whole. More and more companies are adopting customer-centric strategies, programs, tools, and technologies for efficient and effective customer relationship management. They are realizing the need for in-depth and integrated customer knowledge in order to build cooperative relationships with their customers. The customer is at the centre of all business activity and organizations are now desperately trying to restructure their processes around the needs of their strategically significant customers. Customer Relationship Management (CRM) is not just a marketing initiative; it has become a strategic initiative in most companies today. Therefore, the study of CRM has become imperative for aspiring management professionals. The basic purpose of this course is to acquaint students with the conceptual foundations of CRM, examine industry practices, and discuss the relevant issues in the implementation of CRM.

A firm’s most valuable asset is its customers. Organizations need to manage their customer relationships effectively to remain competitive in the interactive era. Thus, this course would attempt to sensitize students to the need for customer-centric strategy as a necessary and important element of managing every successful enterprise. The students would understand how to build profitable relationships with valued customers and how to make managerial decisions that increase the value of a company by increasing the value of the customer base. The overall goals of CRM are to find, attract, and win new clients, nurture and retain those the company already has, and win back former clients. CRM represents an enterprise approach for developing full knowledge about customer behavior and preferences and developing programs and strategies that encourage customers to continually enhance their business relationships with the company
key_takeaways:
• Ability to develop customer-centric strategies across B2B and B2C contexts.
• Framework for measuring CLTV and CRM ROI.
• Understanding of customer acquisition, retention, and loyalty management.
• Skills in key account management and customer-success practices.
• Exposure to social CRM, chatbots, and personalisation through recommendation systems.
prerequisites:
• Core Marketing Management foundations.

• Comfort with numerical analysis for CLTV and ROI calculations.

Includes CLTV economics, customer acquisition, retention, experience management, loyalty programs, and B2B key account management.

description:
Since time immemorial, even before the advent of marketing as a discipline, retail was and had remained an integral part of any business model, being in the forefront of consumer interactions. Retail marketing, as a discipline, hopes to imbibe this age-old system into the paradigms and established structural framework provided by marketing. Retail marketing therefore, leaps a step forward from dwelling into the minds of consumers, to the periphery of their physical cognition --- it is actually the scientific technique that will help the producers finally make it through their produce and reach the consumer. Being the last link in this immensely complicated consumer mind chain, retail is therefore the spearhead, often the make-or-break factor that converts willing or ambivalent customers into eager buyers.

No wonder proponents of retail marketing understand the crucial importance of their subject --- for it deals not only with the veracity of the end users (namely on store psychology, momentary impulses and buying urges, shelf recognition vs. brand recall etc.), but also the existing reasonability of the retailers (such as store layout, shelf arrangement, store efficiency metric etc). A judicious balance between the two can be a daunting task, This is further augmented by the increasing complexity of a changing target --- since the consumer mindset itself is an ever changing terrain, coupled with the target marketing model and product portfolio, it is needless to say that changes in the retail marketing efforts need to be in synch (and often additionally synergetic) to bear the correct fruits of an otherwise long lasting effort on the part of the producer across the value chain.

This course in retail marketing is intended to familiarize the students with the myriad of retail and the techniques retailers often have in their arsenal. The course further tries to highlight established retail practices across markets geographies and different classes of retailers, highlighting the changing retailing landscape (such as advent of digital retail), and ways to deal with them.
key_takeaways:
• Structural understanding of retail management — formats, competition, business models.
• Ability to design retail strategies balancing socio-economic, ethical, and business considerations.
• Framework for merchandise planning, inventory management, and pricing.
• Skills in omnichannel integration and digital retail transformation.
• Capability to evaluate a retailer's strategic position and identify growth opportunities.
prerequisites:
• Core Marketing Management including consumer behaviour and market segmentation.
• Familiarity with branding, pricing, distribution, and promotional strategy.
• Basic awareness of digital commerce trends.

Useful for managers in retail, consumer goods, e-commerce, or any consumer-facing business.

description:
The importance of services as the single largest contributor to the economic growth and GDP of the developed as well as developing economies of the world is widely reported. Apart from expansion of service industry, component of service in all traditional industry is increasing. Services also induce strategic competitive advantage in terms of unique value creation and innovation around traditional products.  Management of service operations focuses on ways and means of delivering service excellence with a view to achieving larger business success. The traditional transformation-process oriented operations management approach may not be adequate to address the issues arising out of interplay with services.  Service operations have unique characteristics which throw additional challenges to managers. Issues such as intangibility, varied degree of direct customer participation, non-inventoriable nature etc. render a lot of traditional Operations Management practices and concepts unusable. Consequently, services management calls for a strong understanding of customers, competitors and firm’s internal processes.

The pedagogy includes lectures, case discussions, guest lectures, simulation and games. The content covers both quantitative and qualitative materials.

Learning Objectives:

1. Explore the relationships between customers’ needs and an organization’s products and services.
2. Explore how managers can influence customers, employees, and service designs to create and capture value, and deliver these services that satisfy customers within the framework of the organization’s objectives and capabilities.
3. Understand how service performance can be improved by studying service delivery and associated management issues.
4. Learning tangible lessons through exposure to wide range of cases, examples and assignments on industries involving different types and aspects of services

• Understanding of service characteristics and service-operations challenges.
• Ability to use service blueprinting and servicescape design.

• Framework for service quality improvement using SERVQUAL gap analysis.

description:
In today’s interconnected world, firms rarely operate within the boundaries of a single country. Products are designed in one region, manufactured in another, assembled elsewhere, and delivered to customers across multiple markets. Global Logistics and Operations lie at the heart of this complex network, enabling firms to coordinate material flows, information, and decisions across borders efficiently and reliably.

Global logistics extends beyond traditional transportation and warehousing decisions. It encompasses international sourcing, global production planning, cross-border distribution, trade compliance, risk management, and sustainability considerations, all while responding to volatile demand, geopolitical uncertainty, and rapid technological change. Operations decisions made in one part of the world increasingly have ripple effects across the entire supply network, making coordination and integration critical managerial capabilities. From an operations perspective, globalization introduces unique challenges related to cost–service trade-offs, lead time variability, capacity allocation, and process standardization versus localization. Managers must design operations systems that balance global efficiency with local responsiveness, often under constraints imposed by infrastructure, regulations, and cultural differences. Logistics performance, therefore, becomes not just an operational concern but a strategic lever for competitive advantage.

This course explores how organizations design, manage, and improve global logistics and operations systems. It integrates analytical tools, managerial frameworks, and real-world cases to help participants understand strategic and tactical decisions in global supply networks. Emphasis is placed on practical decision-making in areas such as network design, transportation strategy, inventory positioning, outsourcing, and resilience building in global operations
key_takeaways:
• Understanding of the integration and coordination challenges in global logistics.
• Ability to evaluate transportation modes and intermodal routing.
• Skills in maritime, road, and multimodal logistics decision-making.
• Framework for international payment and trade compliance.
• Capability to diagnose global disruptions and build logistics resilience.
prerequisites:
• Fundamentals of Operations Management and Quantitative Techniques.
• Data interpretation using Excel.
• Basic understanding of international business helps.
course_curriculum: Development and trends in Logistics
summary: Examines global logistics from an operations perspective — international sourcing, cross-border distribution, trade compliance, risk, and sustainability.
Covers transportation modes (road, rail, maritime, intermodal), payment terms, service response logistics, and global disruption response.
Useful for managers in exports/imports, global manufacturing, international retail, or any cross-border operation.

description:
Operational Excellence is a business management approach that emphasises continuous improvement across all aspects of the business and within all business processes by creating a culture in which management and employees are invested in business outcomes and empowered to implement change. When properly implemented, every member of an organization sees the flow of value to the customer and, if problems arise, finds a solution before any disruptions occur.
When implementing operational excellence within an organization, it can be beneficial to think of the process as a journey rather than a destination. Because the emphasis is on continuous improvement, business leaders and employees should always look for ways to improve their performance.
Organizations must set goals and define metrics in order to determine whether and how they are improving. Automation, process analysis, observability, and data and business management tools can help businesses implement and maintain continuous improvement more quickly.
key_takeaways:
• Theoretical understanding of operational excellence principles.
• Ability to use data and performance metrics for operational decisions.
• Skills in addressing operational challenges creatively and systematically.
• Framework for risk pooling, postponement, and supply-chain innovation.
• Exposure to Industry 4.0 concepts and smart operations.

description:
Operations strategy is the missing link between the operational strategy and the business strategy. It seeks to provide the concepts and suggests approaches to develop competitive advantages for a organization on a long term basis on a sustainable orientation. The subject has a direct and close connection with strategy of resource management. The evolution of the subject is of recent origin and is of high significance in the newly emerged business environment that is volatile, dynamic in changing customer preferences with high emphasis on performance objectives as customization, delivery, flexibility and variety, customer service, speed and more besides the classical performance parameters of cost and quality. The course has been designed to enable the students to prepare, comprehend and analyze operations strategy.

The course is designed to provide an understanding of the role and relevance of operations strategy to an organization and also the basic concepts, techniques and methodologies relevant to operations strategy. Course coverage includes: understanding linkage of OS business strategy, Performance Objectives and operations strategy is developed in decision areas of Capacity, Technology, Supply network, and HR management & Organization Development. The course prepares students to understand the role of core competencies in an organization.

This course explores the major issues and managerial concepts relating to strategic management of the operations function in today's global economy. This course will provide the students with frameworks to link an operations strategy to the firm's competitive positioning as well as tools to understand the economic and technology drivers for a global operations footprint. Business cases studied include a mix from both the manufacturing and service industries. There is a strong global emphasis throughout the course.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to link operations strategy to corporate/business strategy.
• Understanding of performance dimensions: cost, quality, flexibility, delivery, speed.
• Skills in capacity strategy decisions — leading, lagging, and dynamic capability.
• Framework for strategic sourcing using Kraljic matrix and category management.
• Capability to apply DEA for firm performance benchmarking.
prerequisites:
• Fundamentals of Operations Management and Quantitative Techniques are explicit prerequisites.
• Familiarity with strategic management concepts.
• Comfort with quantitative analysis for DEA and learning-curve modelling.
course_curriculum:
Capacity Strategy and Strategic Capacity Management
Dynamic Capacity strategy, Modelling dynamic capacity strategies, guidelines of dynamic capability strategies.
Relative Performance Measurement of Firms/Organizations using the Methodology of Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA)
Improvement Strategy
Product and service development and organization

Covers capacity strategy, learning curves, strategic sourcing, process-technology strategy, and sustainability.
Uses DEA (Data Envelopment Analysis) as the core analytical tool for efficiency benchmarking.

description: This course provides a comprehensive understanding of sustainability and its implications for modern businesses operating in resource-constrained and climate-sensitive environments.
