description:
This course explores the sphere of analytics which is the conversion of data into practical insights, propelling superior business choices. Its main objective is to familiarize students with the complex realm of data-guided supply chain analytics and simulation. The Supply Chain Analytics course aims to equip participants with skills to apply modelling and analytical techniques to analyse various strategic, tactical, and operational supply chain problems. The Simulation theme focuses on key challenges in managing today’s global supply chains, emphasizing supply chain performance, trade- offs in tactical decisions, and Supply Chain Risk Management. The simulation mainly involves exposure to ‘The Fresh Connection,’ a management simulation game focusing on experiential learning aspects like Business simulation games and exercises; Group discussions and presentations; Reflections, Experience sharing, and Experiential Learning. Emphasis will be on applying various descriptive, diagnostic, prescriptive, predictive, and mixed analytical techniques throughout the course.

The theme of this course is two-fold:

1 - A 360-degree view of Supply Chain Analytics applications in the real world. 
2 – Supply Chain Analytics without programming.

The course focuses on the application of Analytics from a business perspective. It focuses on the concepts and the understanding of applications from a practical business implementation. Presentation of the course, therefore, is in an immersive format including case studies and examples from the instructor’s consulting practice. The course borrows many ideas and concepts from other functional subjects to build up understanding among the participants.
key_takeaways:
• Understanding of supply chain analytics across descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive lenses.
• Skills in applying decision trees and random forests to SCM problems.
• Framework for inventory analytics and selective inventory control.
• Capability to design supply-chain contracts (wholesale, buyback, revenue sharing, quantity flexibility).
• Exposure to beer-game simulation for bullwhip and coordination insights.
prerequisites:
• Basic statistics and quantitative techniques.
• Familiarity with supply chain management fundamentals.
• Comfort with data analysis in Excel or R.

Module 1: Introduction
Introduction

Descriptive Analytics

Predictive Analytics & Prescriptive Analytics
Project Presentation
Supply chain coordination

Supplier selection
Analytics for Supply Chain Sustainability

Covers decision trees, random forests, inventory analytics, beer game simulation, and supply-chain contract design.

description: Simulation modelling has become a vital tool in contemporary business world for enabling organizations to analyze complex systems, optimize processes, and improve managerial decision-making. This course provides a hands-on introduction to AnyLogic software, starting with the fundamentals of the software, including its interface, key functionalities, and modeling techniques. students will learn to build simulation models step by step, beginning with simple process flows and gradually advancing to more complex business scenarios. Industries such as manufacturing, logistics, retail, and healthcare leverages AnyLogic software as a tool for business planning, risk assessment, and performance optimization. By completing this course, student will gain the skills needed to develop, test, and implement simulation models that drive data-driven improvements in Business problems. description:
The course Business Models and Business Plans is designed to build the analytical, strategic, and execution-oriented capabilities required to conceptualize, design, and articulate scalable ventures in competitive and uncertain environments. The course provides rigorous, hands-on learning experience in developing effective and growth-oriented business models that create, deliver, and capture value sustainably. Students will engage deeply with contemporary business model frameworks, test assumptions through applied tools, and iteratively refine models to ensure scalability, viability, and long-term strategic alignment.

In parallel, the course equips students with the structured methodology to craft compelling and investment-ready business plans that translate business logic into coherent strategic narratives. Emphasis is placed on financial articulation, risk assessment, competitive positioning, and persuasive communication so that students can design plans that not only guide structured strategic decision-making but also stand out to investors, stakeholders, and funding agencies. Through experiential learning, case analyses, peer review, and project-based development, the course strengthens higher-order competencies including critical thinking, opportunity evaluation, analytical reasoning, and strategic storytelling, enabling students to transform innovative ideas into fundable and growth-ready ventures.
key_takeaways:
• Advanced understanding of contemporary business model frameworks.
• Ability to design and test scalable business models with BMC and Lean approaches.
• Skills for crafting investment-ready business plans.

• Capability to collaborate in teams and pitch business models persuasively.
prerequisites:

• Familiarity with value chain and competitive analysis.
• Comfort with case-based and project-based learning.

Useful for aspiring founders, product managers, consultants, or intrapreneurs who will pitch ventures or initiatives.

course: Chanakya: Strategic Lessons For Today & Tomorrow
description:
We take inspiration from our rich history. History which is full of legendary figures have since ages inspired and motivated us with their exemplary works. It feels good to read about leaders who played important roles in their times. But what if when a person single-handedly shaped the history. A man with various arrows in his quiver, highly regarded and his every word holds same significance as it was centuries before. A universal genius whose areas of expertise included a wide array as a teacher, philosopher, economist, jurist, royal advisor and above all a kingmaker – he can only be Chanakya also known as Kautilya, Vishnugupta and Vishnusharma.

A man so affluent that he transformed a street boy into the emperor of India. If Chanakya transformed the face of politics and economics through his Arthshastra, he has also outlined measures to improve lives of common citizens by his wise words of Chanakya Neeti.

In todays globalized corporate world where nations are in complicated diplomatic relationship, Chanakya can provide a way forward to tomorrows manager. Understanding Chanakya and learning from his treatise will give the students confidence to venture into the competitive corporate world while keeping the national interest and larger good of society in mind.
key_takeaways:
• Understanding of the importance of national interest in corporate strategy.
• Appreciation of the role of geopolitics and geo-economics in strategy.
• Managerial skills drawn from Arthashastra for long-term organisational survival.
• Insight into Chanakya's approach to wealth, negotiation, diplomacy, and empire-building.
• Framework for applying Chanakya Niti in personal and professional life.
prerequisites:
• No formal prerequisite.
• Interest in Indian history, philosophy, and statecraft.
• Openness to integrating ancient texts with modern business thinking.

description:

Traditional entrepreneurship models often fail in such contexts because they prioritize scalability before embeddedness and efficiency before legitimacy.

description:
How the world of corporations has changed ! The 20th-century giants — General Electric, U.S. Steel, General Motors, Ford, Goodyear Tire, and ExxonMobil – have been dislodged by the BigTech giants - Microsoft, Meta (Facebook), Apple, Amazon, Tesla, and Alphabet (Google) – as the world’s most valuable companies today. Beyond statistical appreciation, this tectonic shift has been and will continue to upend industries after industries, and at times decimating the so called established brands for ever. With this background, a holistic understanding of how business platforms originate, evolve, compete, and more often perish, is imperative for managers and entrepreneurs to thrive and survive. It is well-known by now that the rules of game which platform companies rely on vary immensely from those of brick-and-mortar firms. Majority of these companies offer ecosystem that performs as a virtual marketplace for goods and services that these platform companies aren’t necessarily the producer themselves. Demand side economies of scale, also known as network effects that generate winner-take-all or winner-take-most allow one or two platforms to control the market. As opposed to the traditional strategic toolkit that prescribe building a moat around the five forces, platform companies operate through an open ecosystem.

It will be no exaggeration to say that across industries ranging from automotive, banking, education, energy, healthcare, media etc. platform companies are upending the established businesses and society at large. The shift from traditional pipeline business structure to a platform structure has implications for business managers. The practices related to pipeline business-based supply chain, product design, strategy, pricing, technology, leadership and governance may be inadequate to manage the platform business model.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to define platform businesses and predict where they arise.
• Understanding of platform architecture, purpose, and design.
• Framework for monetisation and value creation on platforms.
• Skills for launching platforms and competing against incumbents.
• Awareness of ethics, antitrust, and geopolitical dimensions of platform business.
prerequisites:
• Course workload is higher than standard; class preparedness expected.
• Interest in digital business models and technology strategy.

Platform Demise (DM)
Leadership  and Governance (DM)
Platform Ethics (DM)
Antitrust and Platform (DM)
Platform Techno-Geopolitics (DM)
Guest Session
Wrap up and Project Presentation
summary: Focused on platform business models — pipelines vs platforms, network effects, multisided economics, and platform governance.
Covers launching platforms, pricing, monetisation, ethics, antitrust, and techno-geopolitics.
Case-rich: Upwork, Shopify, Mobike/ofo, Facebook, Airbnb, Google Play, TikTok.
Essential for anyone managing or building platform-based businesses — increasingly the dominant model in digital economy.

course: Game Theory and Strategic Decision Making
description: As a business manager, your decisions have a ripple effect on other businesses, and vice versa. These complex situations are commonly referred to as 'games'. Game Theory is the scientific study of how to play 'games'. You engage in 'games' with competitors, suppliers, customers, partners, and even internal stakeholders. In today's world, decision theory, especially applied game theory, is being integrated with business strategy. This integration allows applied game theorists to develop newer and more relevant theories by drawing on real-life management practices. On the other hand, strategists rely on game theory to provide a general framework for organizing the otherwise overwhelming amount of information available to them. This course is designed to introduce students to the intersection of game theory and strategy, with the goal of enhancing their strategic thinking abilities in complex and interactive environments.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to see business situations as games with identifiable players, payoffs, and strategies.
• Capability to formulate strategies using backward induction and equilibrium analysis.
• Understanding of repeated games, tacit collusion, and the prisoner's dilemma.
• Skills for credible commitment, threats, promises, and bargaining tactics.
• Awareness of asymmetric information, signalling, and auction dynamics.
prerequisites:
• Comfort with basic probability and logical reasoning.
• Core Microeconomics helps with equilibrium concepts.
• No formal prerequisite listed; openness to mathematical reasoning is essential.
course_curriculum:
Repeated games and tacit collusion
Commitment and Strategic Moves - Credibility, threats, and promises
Understanding the value-net Negotiations - Bargaining under complete information. Nash bargaining solution
Tactical issues in negotiating and designing contracts. Investment and hold-up problem
N-person games
Collective action and public policy. Congestion control
Bayesian games: Bayesian updating, signaling, screening
summary: Applies game theory to strategic business situations — competitors, suppliers, customers, partners, and internal stakeholders.
Covers sequential/simultaneous games, repeated games, commitment moves, bargaining, N-person games, auctions, and Bayesian signalling.
Highly relevant for managers in strategy, negotiation, pricing, or any role where actions trigger competitor responses.

description: This course introduces how the global economic environment shapes business strategy and performance. The course begins with a conceptual understanding of trade theories (Mercantilism, Absolute and Comparative Advantage, Heckscher–Ohlin, New trade theory) to explain patterns of specialization, scale economies and national competitiveness. It builds an analytical framework to understand why countries trade, the gains from trade and how global trade is evolving. The course links the economics of international trade, global value chains and market integration to business decisions like market entry, export/import, outsourcing and cross-border partnerships. Students shall learn about the international monetary and financial system, covering exchange-rate regimes, determinants of currency movements, central bank intervention and the balance of payments. The course also examines trade policy instruments (tariffs, quotas, standards, subsidies, export incentives) and the role of multilateral bodies (WTO, IMF, World Bank).
key_takeaways:
• Understanding of why countries trade and the gains from international exchange.
• Ability to interpret trade-policy measures and regional trade agreements.
• Framework for market entry and cross-border investment decisions.
• Grasp of exchange-rate regimes and currency-market dynamics.
• Awareness of global development indicators and macro drivers of business.
prerequisites:
• Basic knowledge of Microeconomics and Macroeconomics.
• Comfort with interpreting data, graphs, and equations.
• Familiarity with basic concepts of Finance helps with monetary-system discussions.
course_curriculum:
International Trade Theories (Why countries trade?)
Heckscher Ohlin Theory, New Trade Theory
Porter's Diamond Model: Competitive Advantage of Nations
International Trade Policies: Tariffs and Quotas
International Trade Agreements and WTO
Trade Blocs and their Implications
International Monetary System
International Financial System, FDI, FPI, and Market Entry Mode
Multinational Corporations (MNCs)
Economic Systems, Economic Cycles, and Global Economic Environment
International Market Entry Strategies
International Business Negotiation and Communication
summary: Links international trade theories (Mercantilism, comparative advantage, Heckscher-Ohlin, New Trade Theory) to business strategy and market entry decisions.
Covers the international monetary and financial system, exchange rates, BoP, and global economic institutions (WTO, IMF, World Bank).
Explores trade policy instruments (tariffs, quotas, subsidies) and their strategic relevance for global business.
Useful for managers in exports/imports, multinational corporations, international strategy, or policy-facing roles.

description: Behavioural Strategy integrates insights from psychology, behavioural economics, and strategic management to understand how managers actually make strategic decisions under uncertainty. The course examines bounded rationality, cognitive biases, leadership cognition, organizational routines, motivation, and decision-making processes that shape strategic choices. It equips students with frameworks to analyse strategic behaviour in firms and apply behavioural insights to improve decision quality and organizational effectiveness.
key_takeaways:
• Strong foundation in behavioural strategy concepts, theories, and phenomena.
• Ability to recognise cognitive biases (anchoring, overconfidence, availability) in real decisions.
• Tools for improving decision quality at individual, group, and organisational levels.
• Understanding of choice architecture and debiasing techniques.
• Framework for coping with uncertainty and unknown unknowns.
prerequisites:

Introduction to behavioural strategy and why does it matter?
Bounded rationality, heuristic and biases in decision making

Decision making in groups and organisation
Choice Architecture: System I &II framework
Coping with uncertainty: Interpretive microfoundations of strategy
How motivation and emotion affect decision making?
Guest session: Top manager decision-making

summary: Integrates psychology, behavioural economics, and strategic management to explain how managers actually decide under uncertainty.

Essential for any manager whose decisions matter — especially those in strategy, leadership, investing, or consulting roles.

course: Nonmarket Strategy: Focus on Geopolitics and Climate Change
description: Given the increased volatility and uncertainty engendered by a multipolar world economy, non-market variables have increasingly become significant inputs driving strategy making and actions of businesses. No longer it is safe and correct for organizations to stay aloof from goings-on as diverse as activism, political risk, regulatory environment, racial discrimination, immigrants’ issue, political lobbying and so on. Seemingly detached from its impact on firm’s business, increasingly these issues are likely to have subtle and not-so subtle implications on bottom line. Put it another way, NMS recognizes that businesses are not just solely economic agents seeking to maximize profits businesses but also operate in the realm of social and political context. Yet, few companies/ managers are equipped with tools and frameworks to developing an effective nonmarket strategy. Stemming from this, our motivation to develop NMS has been our curiosity to understand why some companies despite being a stellar performer in market environment often find it difficult to manoeuvre in non-market context; and in similar vein why some businesses are quite adept at manoeuvring nonmarket environment at ease
key_takeaways:
• Ability to recognise market failures and convert them into business opportunities.
• Capability to design responses to regulatory and civil-society challenges.
• Understanding of state-led capitalism and geopolitical risk management.
• Framework for community relations, corporate lobbying, and crisis management.
• Insight into ESG, carbon markets, and climate-driven business transformation.
prerequisites:
• Core Strategic Management or equivalent grounding in business strategy.
• Basic awareness of global political and regulatory environments.
• No deep technical prerequisite.

Emerging from crisis and regaining trust (SS)

Corporate Lobbying (SS)
ESG & CSR (SS)
Carbon Markets & Net zero (SS)

description: The current workplace is undergoing ever-changing disruptions from emerging technologies and demographic shifts, thereby redefining human behaviour and relationships. How should we flourish and thrive in this complex world? How should we attain and lead well-being (SDG 3) for ourselves and others in the workplace and society? The World Economic Forum has identified 10 essential future skills for 2030, of which 6 are behavioural skills—analytical skills, resilience-flexibility-agility, creative thinking, leadership and social influence, curiosity and lifelong learning, and empathy and active listening. This course aims at enabling participants to learn future skills to flourish and thrive, attain and lead well-being. The course will deliver the mechanism of leading by coaching and mentoring with the objectives of (i) unleashing the hidden potential of individuals and transforming them into performers; (ii) enhancing their well-being and quality of life; and (iii) considering coaching as an alternative career choice.

description: Decoding Team Dynamics is a course designed to help students understand why some teams thrive while others struggle. In today's workplace, complex challenges are solved by teams, not individuals. Yet, most of us have experienced the frustration of dysfunctional teams where meetings go nowhere, voices go unheard, and conflicts fester. This course treats team dynamics not as a mystery but as something that can be systematically decoded by examining communication patterns, role structures, decision-making processes, and cultural norms that shape how teams function. Through real-world cases, hands-on exercises, and evidence-based frameworks, students will learn to diagnose team health, intervene effectively, and become architects of high-performance teams rather than passive participants. Whether you aspire to lead teams, contribute more effectively, or design collaborative organisations, this course equips you with the lens to see what others miss and the tools to build what matters.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to analyse theoretical foundations of team dynamics and apply them in practice.
• Framework for identifying role dynamics and faultlines in teams.
• Skills to distinguish functional from dysfunctional team dynamics.
• Capability to design interventions for dysfunctional teams.
• Transferable insights for leading teams across diverse contexts.
prerequisites:
• Core Organisational Behaviour and HRM.
• Willingness to engage in role-plays, simulations, and peer feedback.
• No technical prerequisite.
course_curriculum:
The Code: What Are We Actually Decoding?
Psychological Safety: The Foundational Cipher
Communication Patterns: Decoding the Real Network
Faultlines & Subgroups: Decoding Splits in the Code
The Hybrid Decoder: Cracking Distance & Digital Nuance
Conflict & Debate: Decoding Clash into Value
Decision-Making: Decoding the Illusion of Consensus
Cultural Codebooks: Decoding Meaning Across Boundaries
The Dynamics of Status & Power: Decoding Influence
Recoding: Designing & Leading for Dynamic Effectiveness
summary: Focused on diagnosing and intervening in team dynamics — psychological safety, communication patterns, faultlines, conflict, status, and power.

description: Negotiation and conflict management are the cornerstones of leadership. In order for managers to lead and administer any organization effectively, they must be able to negotiate with both internal and external constituents in a way and manner that develops or maintains relationships, creates value, and can be utilized cross-culturally. Similarly, managers must be able to identify different types of conflict and manage and harness conflict to make it beneficial to the organizational and personal growth of key constituents. This course covers the key issues and best practices for managers dealing with contentious situations in the workplace. Also, this course provides a space for the exploration of theories and research relating to conflict and negotiation in a range of professional contexts. Students will have opportunities to practice and develop bargaining and negotiation skills in business, employment, and public disputes involving regulatory approvals and community stakeholder negotiations.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to identify and evaluate different strategies for effective negotiation.
• Understanding of substantive, affective, and interpersonal conflict types.
• Skills to manage conflict constructively for organisational and personal growth.
• Capability to apply BATNA and ZOPA in real negotiations.
• Exposure to cross-cultural negotiation and alternative dispute resolution methods.
prerequisites:

Closing the Deal and Post Negotiation Evaluation: agreement template, closing stage, moving past statement, and building a relationship
Presentations of Group Assignments and summing up.

description:
HR analytics, also referred to as people analytics or talent analytics, empowers HR managers and executives to make informed, data-driven decisions regarding their workforce. By leveraging statistics and technology, HR analytics harnesses the untapped potential of employee data, enabling better business decisions and effective workforce management. If we talk about numbers, research by McKinsey shows the people analytics can help in an 80% increase in recruiting efficiency, 25% rise in business productivity and 50% decreases in attrition rate.
This course takes you on a journey, starting from fundamental concepts like calculating mean and progressing to advanced topics such as text analytics and machine learning techniques. There would also be real world cases to help you better understand how data is used to derive people related policies. Upon completion, you'll be equipped to enhance your company's ROI by applying these analytical skills effectively.
key_takeaways:
• Understanding of what HR analytics is and why organisations need it.
• Ability to frame business problems as HR analytics questions.
• Skills in descriptive and inferential HR analytics using Excel, R, and Python.
• Capability to run regression and logistic regression on attrition and performance cases.
• Exposure to text analytics and clustering for HR applications.
prerequisites:
• Basic statistics and familiarity with Excel.
• Comfort with reading HR data.
• Openness to learning Python and R at a basic level.

Introduction to HR analytics, understanding business problem
Univariate analysis in excel, Python (real world HR problems)
Discussion of case
Bivariate analysis in excel, Python
Case discussion
Hypothesis testing using p value, one sample t test, paired t-test, chi-square, ANOVA on various HR problems in Python, R
Linear regression on real world case
Logistic regression on real world attrition case
Text analytics and clustering
Final wrap up

Covers univariate and bivariate analysis, hypothesis testing, regression, logistic regression, and text analytics/clustering.
Case-rich with Cynet Systems, Apturja Power, Money Cash Flow Inc., ScaleneWorks, and Barney.

description:
In today's evolving work environment, characterized by an intricate mix of hybrid working models, a diverse workforce, and highly personalized adjustments, managers must grasp the nuances of human relationships. They need to achieve success and preserve top talent by relating to human interactions, forecasting others' behaviour, and crafting their strategies accordingly. This approach not only enhances team dynamics but also ensures a cohesive and productive work atmosphere where every individual feels valued and understood.

This course is designed to highlight the psychology behind human relationships and their impact on strategic decision making. The course embeds key concepts of interpersonal dynamics, transactional analysis, and the science of decision-making. It primarily explores the cognitive patterns that individuals adopt due to the predisposition of various thoughts, which ultimately guide people's choices and actions in given situations. Within this framework, multiple decision-makers' choices significantly transform human interactions, leading to a diverse range of outcomes in the workplace. The course seeks to introduce students to the underlying theories that explain these choices and offer strategies for navigating complex situations during critical times.

By engaging with this course, participants will gain a deeper understanding of human psychological processes and how these adapt to both social and physical environments. This knowledge will enable them to make more accurate predictions about others' behaviour and enhance their own strategic decision-making processes. Through this enriched understanding, the course aims to equip individuals with the tools necessary for effective negotiation, problem-solving, and leadership in a variety of settings.
key_takeaways:
• Understanding of the theory behind people's games and why they matter.
• Ability to identify the role of emotions, communication, and power in interpersonal games.
• Capability to predict others' behaviour and strategise decisions accordingly.
• Framework for navigating workplace politics constructively.
• Exposure to Eric Berne's ego states and transactional analysis.
prerequisites:
• Basic understanding of Organisational Behaviour and Managerial Communication.
• Openness to psychometric assessments and interpersonal exercises.
• No technical prerequisite.

What do people want?
Eric Berne’s psychology behind games
Guest session: How to navigate political landscape at workplace and build networks/ relationships (inside and outside)?
Levels of reasoning in politics at work
Are we competing/ cooperating?
Experimental research presentation
Are we frenemies?
Mad, Glad & Sad about games
Group Project presentation
summary: Explores the psychology of human relationships and interpersonal dynamics using Eric Berne's Transactional Analysis and game theory.
Covers workplace politics, emotional intelligence, organisational influence, and the cognitive patterns that shape decision-making.

description:
Leading with Cultural Agility course prepares students for the reality of modern leadership: the ability to adapt their approach across cultural boundaries, national, organisational, generational, and professional without losing authenticity or effectiveness. Through practical frameworks like The Culture Map and Cultural Intelligence (CQ), you will learn to decode the cultural logic beneath
behaviour, expand your repertoire of leadership responses, and make deliberate choices about how to show up in different contexts. The course combines self-assessment, real-world case studies, cross-cultural simulations, and peer learning to build both self-awareness and adaptive capability. The course will equip the students with the confidence and toolkit to lead diverse teams, navigate global negotiations, and build inclusive organisational cultures, essential capabilities for anyone leading in today's interconnected world.

description: The aim of this course is to introduce you to the concept of change management and organizational transformation, from the people side of the business. To determine what, why and how change has evolved over time, we will understand the macro-level antecedents of the contemporary business landscape (e.g., continuing globalization, increasing convergence between industries, megatrends, continuous innovation, and disruptive technologies), and their subsequent impact on organizational development. The course will expose the intricacies of managing change at organizational, group, and individual level. And how to integrate change towards organizational transformation and use change leadership techniques. The course is designed to develop theoretical, conceptual, and practical understanding of managing change and transformation.  The broad domain of organizational change falls within the larger context of organization studies.

Case-based teaching with diagnostic exercises and group projects.

complementary_courses.why: Technology-side complement to the people-side focus here.
Strategic-level view of transformation.
Same faculty — interpersonal dynamics during change.

description:
Performance management is a strategic tool that enables organisations to maximise employee performance, transforming every interaction into a valuable learning opportunity. Effective performance management fosters a culture of continuous growth, improvement, and excellence. Compensation management plays a vital role in attracting, retaining, and motivating top talent. By designing and implementing competitive and equitable total rewards programs, organisations can drive business outcomes, enhance employee engagement, and establish a strong employer brand.

In this comprehensive course, students will acquire the essential skills and knowledge required to excel in performance and compensation management. Key takeaways include coaching for performance and development, conducting constructive conversations with employees, supporting employee growth and development, and designing effective total rewards programs. By the end of this course, students will be equipped to drive business success through strategic performance and compensation management.
key_takeaways:
• Ability to define employee goals and align them with company objectives.
• Skills in coaching, feedback, and performance conversations.
• Framework for developing compensation philosophy and conducting job evaluations.
• Capability to design equitable pay structures and variable pay.
• Awareness of DE&I issues in compensation and pay transparency.
prerequisites:
• Organisational Behaviour and HRM core courses.
• Basic understanding of strategic HR.
• No quantitative prerequisite.
course_curriculum:
Introduction to Performance Management
Setting Performance Goals and Objectives: Understanding SMART goals, aligning goals with company objectives, and writing effective performance goals.
Creating Performance Development Plans
Coaching and Feedback
Managing Performance Issues
Conducting Performance Evaluations
Creating a Performance Management System
Introduction to Compensation Management and underlying theories
Compensation Strategy and Philosophy
Job Evaluation and Analysis
Compensation Structure and Administration
Compensation and Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion: Understanding the impact of compensation on diversity, equity, and inclusion, including pay equity and transparency.
Compensation Trends and Technology
Final Project Presentation
summary: Covers performance management (goal setting, coaching, feedback, evaluations, improvement plans) and compensation management (philosophy, job evaluation, structure, DE&I, trends).
Practical focus on building skills as a coach and designing effective total-rewards programs.
Heavy case and exercise load: SeaCloud, Afreximbank, Tata Motors, Kaymak Consulting.
Essential for HR managers, line managers, and anyone managing people or designing rewards systems.

complementary_courses.why: Upstream talent frameworks that feed performance management.
complementary_courses.faculty: Prof. Atri Sengupta & Dr. Dinesh Murugesan

Same faculty — team-level foundation for performance.

description: Technology is playing an increasingly dominant role in the financial services industry, empowering existing players and threatening to obliviate business models of entire sectors within the industry. The course exposes students to this fast-growing and exciting intersection between technology and finance – P2P lending, Loan processing, Blockchains, crypto currency, Bitcoins, electronic payments, financial applications of machine learning, robo-advising, algorithmic trading, digital transformation in banks, central bank digital currency, new approaches to KYC and AML, regulation of Fintech and fintech venture funding and other topics.
key_takeaways:
1. Fintech industry overview and ecosystem
2. P2P Lending – mechanisms, credit risk, and regulation
3. Cryptography and encryption algorithms
4. Blockchain / Distributed Ledger Technology and its applications
5. Crypto currency and Bitcoin
6. Initial Coin Offerings (ICO, STO, IEO)
7. Electronic payments and fintech payment models
8. Robo-advising and its impact on wealth management
9. Machine learning, Algorithmic trading and High-Frequency Trading (HFT)
10. Digital transformation in banks
11. Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs)
12. AML and KYC – new approaches
13. InsurTech – concepts, applications, and business models
14. RegTech – regulatory technology and SupTech
15. Disruptive Technologies (Quantum computing, DAPPs)
16. Project evaluation for Fintech ventures and start-up funding (VC, crowdfunding)
prerequisites: Nil
course_curriculum:
Session 1: Overview of the Fintech Industry — What is Fintech, Evolution from E-Finance to Fintech, Major impact factors, Fintech ecosystem and business models, Challenges for Fintech sector
Session 2: P2P Lending Market — Mechanism of P2P lending, Credit risk, Managing risk for investors, Crowd funding vs P2P lending, Regulation
Session 3: Cryptography and Encryption Algorithms — Secret and Public key cryptography, RSA and Hash Function
Session 4: Blockchain – Distributed Ledger Technology — How Blockchain works, Properties and Limitations
Session 5: Loan Processing from a P2P Platform — Case Discussion: Lending Club; Credit Ease: Taking Inclusive Finance Online
Session 6: Blockchain Applications — BFSI, Trade Finance, Ripple, Cobalt DL, BCT in Capital markets, R3
Session 7: Blockchain Applications (Contd.) — Case Discussion: Deutsche Bank: Pursuing Blockchain Opportunities
Session 8: Crypto Currency and Bitcoin
Session 9: Initial Coin Offerings (ICO) — ICO, STO and IEO, Regulation of ICO; Case: Filecoin Initial Coin Offering
Session 10: Electronic Payments — E-Payment methods, Machine learning for E-Payment, Paytm; Case: Stripe: Helping Money Move on the Internet
Session 11: Robo-Advising — Impact on wealth management, Robo-advisory services; Case: Charles Schwab Corp.
Session 12: Machine Learning and Financial Applications — ML and AI in finance, Hedge Fund strategies
Session 13: Algorithmic Trading and HFT — Algo trading, Market microstructure, Algo analysis strategies and tools
Session 14: InsurTech — Fundamental concepts, Key applications, Business models
Session 15: RegTech — Technologies used, Regtech at HSBC, SupTech and Fintech
Session 16: Digital Transformation in Banks — Digital disruption and Banking Response
Session 17: Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs) — Opportunities, Benefits, Implementation
Session 18: New Approaches to AML and KYC — Money Laundering, CKYC, Blockchain-based KYC
Session 19: Case Discussion – DBS: From the "World's Best Bank" to Building the Future Ready Enterprise
Session 20: Disruptive Technologies, Project Evaluation, Start-up Funding — Quantum computing, DAPPs, Real option analysis, VC and crowdfunding

complementary_courses.why: DLT and smart contracts are core FinTech infrastructure; this course deepens the blockchain foundations covered in the FinTech module on distributed ledger applications and crypto ecosystems.
Bridges FinTech's coverage of robo-advising and algorithmic trading with hands-on AI/ML financial modelling tools, extending the quantitative dimension of the course into applied analytics.
complementary_courses.course: AI for Financial Analytics and Decision Making

complementary_courses.why: Digital financial services introduce novel cyber, credit, and liquidity risks; this course equips students to build enterprise risk frameworks directly applicable to FinTech business models.

course: Macroeconomics for Business and Financial Strategy
description: Macroeconomic conditions significantly influence strategic decisions across finance, marketing, operations, and investment. Changes in interest rates, inflation, exchange rates, fiscal policy, labour-market conditions, and financial sentiment affect pricing, valuation, capital allocation, and long-term planning. This course provides a practical and analytical understanding of how macroeconomic developments and financial-market dynamics shape business strategy and investment outcomes. Students will learn to interpret macroeconomic indicators, assess policy actions, and evaluate their implications for firms, industries, and financial markets. Particular emphasis is placed on reading and applying major policy documents, such as the Economic Survey, the Union Budget, the RBI Monetary Policy Reports, and IMF outlooks, in managerial and financial contexts.
key_takeaways:
1. Interpret macroeconomic and financial-market indicators for strategic business and investment decisions
2. Diagnose economic turning points using leading, coincident, and lagging indicators
3. Evaluate GDP, inflation, employment, and development indicators critically
4. Assess the implications of monetary and fiscal policy for firms, sectors, and markets
5. Analyse exchange-rate movements and global macro spillovers
6. Understand the role of human development, inequality, and labour-market dynamics in shaping demand and competitiveness
7. Apply macro-financial reasoning to equity valuation, sector rotation, and top-down investment strategies
8. Communicate macro-based strategic insights using data dashboards, memos, and presentations
prerequisites: MBA202: Macroeconomic Analysis
course_curriculum:
Session 1: Introduction to Macroeconomic Strategy — Role of macroeconomics in managerial and investment decisions, macro-financial linkages, markets as forward-looking indicators
Sessions 2–3: Business Cycles and Economic Indicators — Economic regimes, business cycles; leading, coincident, and lagging indicators; yield curve, PMI, credit, unemployment, inflation; macro dashboard construction
Sessions 4–5: National Income and Growth Analytics — GDP measurement approaches, real vs nominal GDP, output gaps, growth accounting, productivity, and earnings linkages
Session 6: Human Development and Structural Change — HDI and welfare metrics, human capital, structural transformation, demographic transition, long-run market attractiveness
Session 7: Income Inequality and Labour Markets — Inequality measures, skill premiums, wage differentials, labour productivity, inclusive growth and implications for demand
Sessions 8–9: Inflation and Pricing Decisions — Headline and core inflation, inflation drivers, expectations, pricing power, pass-through, elasticity, and pricing strategy
Sessions 10–11: Monetary Policy and Financial Conditions — Monetary transmission, interest-rate and credit channels, financial conditions, QE/QT, YCC, and negative interest rate regimes
Sessions 12–13: Fiscal Policy and Sovereign Risk — Fiscal accounting, budget indicators, deficits, fiscal multipliers, crowding out, debt sustainability, sovereign credibility
Sessions 14–15: Exchange Rates and External Sector Dynamics — NEER/REER, PPP, competitiveness, Marshall-Lerner, J-Curve, overshooting, capital flows
Sessions 16–17: Macro and Equity Valuation — Discount rates, cost of equity, ERP, equity duration, rate sensitivity, growth vs value, sector rotation, macro investing
Sessions 18–19: Financial Crises and Market Dislocations — Credit cycles, coordination failures, bank runs, leverage build-up, liquidity spirals, systemic risk indicators, crisis transmission
Session 20: Course Integration and Strategic Synthesis — Integrating macro indicators, policy, and markets into a strategic framework for managerial and investment decision-making

complementary_courses.why: Game theory models complement macro equilibrium analysis; both courses apply quantitative reasoning to competitive and strategic decision-making.
complementary_courses.course: Game Theory and Strategic Decision Making

complementary_courses.why: Extends macro open-economy analysis into trade theory, global value chains, and cross-border investment strategy.
Translates macro variables—interest rates, FX, inflation—into enterprise risk frameworks for hedging and capital management.

description: We are living through a period of sweeping change in how brands reach and influence people. The proliferation of platforms, the rise of influencers, the fragmentation of attention, the power of data and personalisation — all of it has fundamentally altered the communication landscape. And yet, the more the tools change, the more the fundamentals matter. A powerful insight, a clear brand idea, a well-crafted brief, and a message that earns its place in people's lives have not gone out of fashion. If anything, they are harder to get right in a noisy digital world, and more valuable when you do. This course is built around that tension. Students will learn to embrace the possibilities of digital communication without losing sight of what has always made communication work — thinking like a brand strategist, briefing like an agency planner, and evaluating like someone who understands both the art and the numbers. The course follows the real-world process of building an IMC campaign: understanding the audience, developing the brand idea, choosing the right media and message, and measuring what works. Students work on a live brand project from day one, pitching a full IMC campaign at the end of the course.
key_takeaways:
1. Apply a strategic planning process to develop integrated marketing communication campaigns tied to business objectives
2. Uncover and articulate consumer insights using immersive research methods including observation, ethnography, and cultural analysis
3. Build a brand communication platform (the big idea) that connects the brand to its audience with consistency and resonance
4. Write effective creative briefs that inspire memorable, meaningful, and motivating work from agency partners
5. Make informed media decisions across traditional, digital, and emerging platforms — understanding not just reach but relevance
6. Evaluate communication effectiveness against brand, marketing, and business metrics
7. Recognise the ethical responsibilities that come with the power of marketing communication
prerequisites: None
course_curriculum:
Session 1: The Integrated Approach to Marketing Communication — Why integration matters, customer journey as organising principle, business and communication objectives
Session 2: Segmentation, Targeting & Positioning as the Foundation of Communication — STP through the communication lens, identifying the right audience for each touchpoint
Session 3: Understanding the Brand — Brand equity and communication effectiveness, developing a Brand Essence Statement
Session 4: Consumer Behaviour and the Communication Process — Attention, emotion, memory; models of communication; low vs high-involvement decisions
Session 5: Culture, Category, and Consumer Insight — Ethnography, observation, semiotics; turning a consumer truth into a communication insight
Session 6: The Brand Idea — What is a "big idea" and why it matters; brand archetypes; connecting culture, category, and brand
Sessions 7–8: Communication Strategy and the Briefing Process — Anatomy of a great creative brief; writing briefs that inspire; workshop exercise
Sessions 9–10: Creative Strategy — What makes communication memorable, meaningful, and motivating; brand storytelling; evaluating creative work
Session 11: Media Planning and Strategy — Setting media objectives, reach and frequency, media as message, selecting and optimising across platforms
Session 12: Thinking Communication in a Digital Age — Personalised and always-on communication; search, social, video, audio, and OTT; branded content
Session 13: Influencer Marketing and Community-Led Communication — Types and fit; celebrity endorsement vs micro-influencers; co-creating with communities
Session 14: Public Relations, Corporate Communication & CSR — Reputation management, corporate advertising, stakeholder communication, crisis communication
Session 15: Sales Promotion, Sponsorships, Activations, and Experiential Communication — Role in IMC mix, consumer vs trade promotions, risk of over-reliance
Session 16: Measuring Campaign Effectiveness — Pre-testing, tracking, post-evaluation; brand metrics vs business metrics
Session 17: IMC in B2B Contexts — What changes, what stays the same in business-to-business communication
Session 18: Ethics in Marketing Communication — Stereotyping, manipulation, misinformation; inclusivity and sustainability in IMC
Session 19: Connecting the Dots — Full-campaign simulation: brief to media plan to creative
Session 20: Project Presentations and Course Wrap-Up — Group IMC campaign pitches, peer and faculty Q&A

complementary_courses.why: IMC execution depends on a coherent brand platform; Brand Management provides the brand strategy and equity foundations that IMC brings to life across channels.
complementary_courses.faculty: Prof. Poonam Kumar & Dr. Anurag Dugar

complementary_courses.why: Deep consumer insight is the engine of great communication; this course provides the psychological and behavioural foundation for developing resonant IMC campaigns.
complementary_courses.faculty: Prof. Nitin Soni & Prof. Poonam Kumar

complementary_courses.why: Digital thinking is woven throughout modern IMC; deepens expertise in digital channels, analytics, and paid/owned/earned media strategies central to any contemporary campaign.

description: Agile product development and project management have become essential capabilities for organizations navigating digital transformation and AI-driven innovation. Traditional project management approaches are increasingly inadequate in environments characterized by uncertainty, rapid technological change, and evolving customer expectations. Agile methodologies, particularly Scrum, enable iterative development, faster feedback loops, and enhanced collaboration across cross-functional teams. This course provides a comprehensive understanding of Agile principles, Scrum frameworks, and Lean thinking, with a strong focus on their application in developing digital and AI-enabled solutions. It bridges theory with practice through case studies, hands-on exercises, and discussions on emerging technologies such as GenAI, RPA, and intelligent workflow orchestration. The course emphasizes practical implementation in both IT and non-IT contexts, including manufacturing and supply chain systems.
key_takeaways:
1. Understand the fundamental concepts and evolution of Agile methodologies
2. Develop an Agile mindset for managing uncertainty and complexity in projects
3. Differentiate Agile from traditional project management approaches such as Waterfall
4. Understand Scrum roles, events, and artifacts in project execution
5. Apply Lean principles such as MVP, Kanban, rapid prototyping, and continuous feedback
6. Explore the integration of Agile and Lean in developing digital and AI-based solutions
7. Analyse the role of emerging technologies (GenAI, RPA, control towers) in enabling organisational agility
8. Develop practical skills in backlog prioritization, user story development, and Agile execution tools
9. Evaluate real-world Agile applications through case studies in operations and digital transformation
prerequisites: None
course_curriculum:
Sessions 1–2: The Concept and Fundamentals of Agile — Brief history of Agile, enabling agility in a manufacturing world, key principles and pillars, Agile mindset, systems enabling agility in supply chain; Chapter review on Strategic Supply Chain Management
Sessions 3–4: Agile Project Management — Agile project lifecycle, difference from Waterfall model, roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team), concepts of Epic/feature/user story/backlog prioritization; Lean concepts (MVP, Kanban, rapid prototyping, learn fast/fail fast); Article review: Gen AI Robots are reshaping Services
Sessions 5–6: Agile Execution and Scrum — Scrum framework, sprint planning, daily scrum, sprint review and retrospection, iterative development and feedback loop, adoption of Agile in non-IT projects; Hands-on activity: user story grooming, backlog prioritization (MoSCoW, WSJF), burndown chart
Sessions 7–8: Lean and Agile in AI Solutions — AI solution implementation with Lean and Agile, business preparedness, RPA, workflow automation, simulations, control towers, Conversational AI, Agentic AI, and Intelligent workflow orchestration; Article review: Customer experience in the age of AI
Sessions 9–10: Case Study Discussion, Presentation and Quiz

complementary_courses.why: Provides traditional PM frameworks that Agile complements; understanding Waterfall and PMBOK makes the shift to Agile thinking more deliberate and contextual.
complementary_courses.faculty: Prof. Rohit Gupta & Mr. Aasheesh Dixit

complementary_courses.why: Scales Agile execution to enterprise-level digital transformation strategy; explores how Agile enables organisational change in tech-driven environments.
Applies Agile and Lean principles to supply chain digitalization; extends the course's non-IT Agile context into logistics and operations domains.
complementary_courses.faculty: Prof. Harshad Sonar & Prof. Kaustov Chakraborty

description: Applied Optimization and Analytics (AOA) is an operations-domain focused elective that extends the LP foundations of Quantitative Techniques 2 into six advanced Operations Research (OR) domains: Foundations and Integer Programming, Transportation and Assignment Problems, Network Optimization Models, Mixed Integer & Binary Programming, Nonlinear Programming & Metaheuristics, and Game Theory & Decision Analysis. The course is intentionally focused — each topic is taught with sufficient depth for management students to confidently formulate, solve, and interpret real OR models without specialist assistance. Computational work is developed in parallel using Microsoft Excel (Solver Add-in) and Python (PuLP, NetworkX, SciPy), ensuring students graduate with both interpretability and scalability in their analytical toolkit.
key_takeaways:
Module I – Foundations of OR: Simplex formulation, Duality and Post-Optimal Analysis, Sensitivity Analysis for resource allocation, product mix, and budgeting decisions
Module II – Transportation & Assignment Problems: North-West Corner, Vogel's, MODI methods; Hungarian Algorithm for assignment; applications in machine-task allocation and job scheduling
Module III – Network Optimization Models: Dijkstra's shortest path, Minimum Spanning Tree, Maximum Flow, and Minimum Cost Flow for supply chain and logistics
Module IV – Mixed Integer & Binary Programming: Branch-and-Bound, Branch-and-Cut; facility location, capital budgeting, and crew scheduling problems using Excel Solver and Python PuLP
Module V – Nonlinear Programming & Metaheuristics: KKT conditions, Quadratic and Convex Programming, Simulated Annealing, and Genetic Algorithms for large combinatorial problems
Module VI – Game Theory & Decision Analysis: Decision trees, utility theory, Bayesian updating, and game-theoretic concepts applied to competitive strategy and investment under uncertainty
prerequisites:
Quantitative Techniques 2 (Term II) — Linear Programming foundations assumed. 
Familiarity with Microsoft Excel. 
No prior Python knowledge required.
course_curriculum:
MODULE I — FOUNDATIONS OF OR (Sessions 1–4): Simplex Formulation, Duality and Post-Optimal Analysis; Applications: resource allocation, product mix, budgeting, sensitivity-based managerial decisions; Case: Managing Linen at Apollo Hospitals
MODULE II — TRANSPORTATION & ASSIGNMENT PROBLEMS (Sessions 5–8): Initial Basic Feasible Solution (NW Corner, Least Cost, Vogel's); Transportation Simplex (MODI/UV Method); Assignment Problem: formulation as 0-1 IP; Hungarian Algorithm; Applications: Machine-Task Allocation, Job Scheduling, Sales Territory Assignment; Case: Merton Truck Co.
MODULE III — NETWORK OPTIMIZATION MODELS (Sessions 9–10): Graphs, Nodes, Arcs and Flow Concepts; Shortest Path (Dijkstra's); Minimum Spanning Tree; Maximum Flow and Minimum Cost Flow; Applications: Supply Chain Routing, Project Networks; Case: DHL Global Forwarding
MODULE IV — MIXED INTEGER & BINARY PROGRAMMING (Sessions 11–13): Integer Programming formulation (Pure IP, Binary IP, Mixed IP); Branch-and-Bound and Branch-and-Cut Approach; Applications: Facility location, capital budgeting, crew scheduling; Case: Agarwal Automobiles: Fuel Station Forecasting
MODULE V — NONLINEAR PROGRAMMING & METAHEURISTICS (Sessions 14–17): Foundations of NLP; Quadratic, Separable and Convex Programming; Introduction to Metaheuristics; Simulated Annealing and Genetic Algorithms; Applications: Pricing, portfolio optimization, nonlinear cost planning
MODULE VI — GAME THEORY & DECISION ANALYSIS (Sessions 18–20): Game Theory (simple games vs mixed strategies); Decision Making under Uncertainty; Decision Trees and Utility Theory; Applications: Competitive strategy, negotiation, investment under uncertainty, risk-based managerial decisions

complementary_courses.why: Provides the foundational analytics and modelling mindset that AOA extends into advanced OR domains; covers descriptive and predictive analytics as a precursor.
Direct application domain for OR models taught in AOA; transportation, network flow, and optimization methods are applied to real supply chain problems.
Builds the quantitative decision-making foundation—LP, probability, and statistical methods—that AOA extends into advanced OR and metaheuristics.

description: This course introduces students to the field of corporate entrepreneurship and innovation, and familiarizes them with the tools, methods, and logic that enable participation in or leadership of successful corporate entrepreneurship (CE) and innovation programs. For an increasing number of established and large companies, it is becoming difficult to predict the future and manage growth from existing businesses. Maturing technologies and aging product portfolios require established companies to create, develop, and sustain innovative new initiatives. Corporate entrepreneurship and innovation is being adopted by organizations to address this challenge through creation of new businesses, as well as rewriting the dominant logic of existing businesses for strategic renewal. If organizations wish to avoid becoming obsolete (like NOKIA), building capabilities of entrepreneurship and innovation is essential. The course acquaints students with theoretical issues in corporate entrepreneurship, managing innovation processes, and strategic renewal of corporations, while building entrepreneurial orientation, innovative thinking, and knowledge of managing CE and innovation programs.
key_takeaways:
1. Knowledge of how organizations of different types, operating in varied contexts, survive, stay competitive, and grow using corporate entrepreneurship and innovation
2. Understanding of opportunities and challenges for firms while managing entrepreneurship and innovation in established organizations
3. Innovative thinking mindset for organizational excellence
4. Practical exposure to corporate accelerators, corporate venture capital, and design thinking as tools for scaling innovation
5. Ability to evaluate and develop corporate entrepreneurship strategies drawing on real-world case studies
prerequisites: Good competency in Strategic Management core course
course_curriculum:
Session 1: Competitive Advantage through Innovation and Entrepreneurship — Managing an ambidextrous organization: balancing innovation and efficiency
Sessions 2–3: Idea to Entrepreneurship; New Models of Managing Innovation — P&G's new model for innovation; Guardrails in Managing Technology and Innovation; Cases: XYZ Corporation, Corning Inc. Technology Strategy in 2003
Sessions 4–5: Design Thinking and Innovation — Why Design Thinking Works (Jeanne Liedtka, HBR 2018); Workshop
Sessions 6–7: Initiating and Scaling Corporate Entrepreneurship Activities — Ecosystem Advantage: Harnessing the Power of Partners; Cases: HCL Technologies: Fuel for Growth, HCL Technologies: Driving Innovation Through Ecosystem
Sessions 8–9: Corporate Accelerators and Corporate Venture Capital — Power of corporate accelerators; Corporate venture capital strategy; Cases: Intel Capital: The Berkeley Networks Investment
Session 10: Group Projects and Course Review — Group Presentations

complementary_courses.why: Corporate ventures require new business model thinking; this course deepens the ability to design, stress-test, and pitch the business models that CE initiatives produce.
Complements the corporate context with independent and social venture creation; expands the entrepreneurship toolkit from internal innovation to market-creating initiatives.
Corporate entrepreneurship is fundamentally about transforming established organizations; provides the change management and transformation leadership skills needed to execute CE programs.
